title: Methodological Notes on Two Rater-Effect Simulation Studies
text:
Preface
This is methodological feedback on a doctoral student’s dissertation in rater-effect measurement, completed in an educational-research doctoral program at the University of Alabama — working notes on the two simulation studies at its methodological core, kept for the record and for future reference, with a view toward a journal manuscript developed from them. The candidate and the advisor are not named; the dissertation is discussed throughout in the third person. These are analytical notes about its methods — not a verdict on the dissertation, and not a letter to anyone.

title: Methodological Notes on Two Rater-Effect Simulation Studies
text:
Scope
What follows is not an appeal against the degree, and it does not claim the dissertation is broken. The PRISMA systematic review in the dissertation’s Chapter 2 is a genuine piece of scholarship — principled in its coding, thorough in its coverage, and useful for situating rater-modeling choices in the wider literature. The decision to keep Many-Facet Rasch Model (MFRM) and Hierarchical Rater Model (HRM) indicators conceptually distinct across every analysis was the sound disciplinary instinct, and it holds throughout. The dissertation is complete and defensible as submitted. Nothing here reopens that question.
The point is narrower and forward-looking: the two simulation studies — the dissertation’s Chapters 3 and 4, its methodological core — can be made considerably stronger before they become journal articles. A committee reasonably extends a candidate the benefit of the doubt; a peer reviewer does not. The distance between a defensible dissertation and a bulletproof manuscript is the territory these notes cover. Closing it is ordinary revision, not a failure of the original work.

title: Methodological Notes on Two Rater-Effect Simulation Studies
text:
The through-line
Every chapter returns to one organizing question:

The concern is almost never that a limitation went unmentioned; it is whether the numbers that are reported can carry the interpretation placed on them.

The dissertation was, in fact, conscientious about limitations — it acknowledges several of the very issues examined here in depth. So this is not a hunt for unstated caveats. The pressure is on the inference: given the model as specified and the design as implemented, do the reported estimates mean what the conclusions take them to mean. That is the whole of it.

title: Methodological Notes on Two Rater-Effect Simulation Studies
text:
On the checks
Where these notes run a numerical check, they implement the dissertation’s own equations from scratch in R; they do not re-run TAM or immer. The claim is never that the software is wrong. It is narrower: here is how the specified model behaves, worked through the same mathematics, so that the model and its reported behavior are compared on the same numbers. Each verification — and the Python that cross-checked every derived value — starts from the equations as written in the dissertation. The checks are an exercise in diligence, not an attempt to catch the software out.

title: Methodological Notes on Two Rater-Effect Simulation Studies
text:
How to read these notes
The Executive Summary (immediately following) lists every concern on a single page, sorted by severity, so the whole landscape is visible before any one chapter. Seven chapters then follow, each taking one issue through a four-part path:

What the dissertation does
Why it threatens the conclusion
What would fix it
What a reviewer will ask

The chapters run in reading order — hardest-to-defend first: the HRM’s ψ identification problem and the collapsing severe-rater location lead, because those are the two places a careful reviewer is most likely to stop before reaching the Discussion. The closing Revision Roadmap runs in revision order — highest-leverage first, sequencing the fixes by leverage and logical dependency rather than by reading difficulty (the Executive Summary’s “Where to start” explains why the two orders differ). A Reproducibility Appendix holds all of the R code and points to the Python that double-checked every number.
Two box types recur, and are worth knowing in advance:






TipFix-it



Green boxes hold the specific, minimal change recommended — no more than what is needed to address the concern at hand.








WarningWhat a reviewer will ask



Amber boxes rehearse the exact question a measurement-journal reviewer is likely to pose — in the voice they would use.


Code blocks are folded by default; expand them only to trace a calculation step by step.

title: Executive summary
text:
The whole book turns on one sentence:

My concern is almost never that a limitation went unmentioned; it is whether the numbers that are reported can carry the interpretation placed on them.

Below is every concern on one page, ordered hardest-to-defend first. Each links to a full chapter with the four-part treatment (what the dissertation does → why it threatens the conclusion → what would fix it → what a reviewer will ask). The dissertation is complete and defensible as it stands; this is a map for the journal version of Studies 2 and 3.



Table 1: Every concern, ordered by how hard it is to defend.












#
Concern
Severity
What is at stake (one verified number)
The one-line fix




1
The HRM’s ψ is not identified for misfit raters
🔴 Critical · must-ask
For a noise rater the profile likelihood is flat above ψ ≈ 5, so the reported ψ ≈ 16–32 are not identified — weakly-determined summaries, not estimates (ψ = 30 sets the SDM spread parameter to ~7× the width of the 0–4 scale)
Treat misfit ψ as non-estimable; log-scale + regularizing prior; report ESS/MCSE and prior sensitivity


2
The severe-rater location collapses with sample size
🔴 Critical · must-ask
Reported severe location falls 1.61 → 0.62 → 0.26 as N grows, while an independent MFRM recovers a stable ≈ 1.5–2.0 — pointing to a pipeline/centering issue rather than a real effect (an audit would confirm)
Report parameter recovery (bias/RMSE/coverage) per condition; separate the sample-size confound; audit the centering


3
The “misfit” manipulation is uniform noise
🔴 Critical
Setting the slope to 0 makes every category equally likely (corr with ability ≈ 0) — uniform noise (on average), not realistic inaccuracy; and the MFRM has a built-in home-field advantage
Use a range of realistic inaccuracy mechanisms; adopt a neutral cross-generative design


4
A single short MCMC chain carries the HRM
🔴 Critical
700 draws from one chain ⇒ R-hat undefined and the MCSE of φ (≈ 0.085–0.19) is the size of the between-condition differences interpreted (0.05–0.16)
≥ 4 chains, longer run; report R-hat/ESS/MCSE for focal parameters; prior sensitivity; posterior-predictive checks


5
“Sensitivity” was never measured as detection
🟠 Fundamental · framing
Defined as detection (p. 48) but measured as the partial η² of means on effect raters only; a big η² hides a 16% false-positive rate, and η² = .01 (“practical”) ≈ chance detection (AUC ≈ 0.56, where 0.5 is a coin flip)
Report TPR/FPR/ROC-AUC at the cut + recovery; include normal and effect raters; reserve “sensitivity” for detection


6
Local dependence is marginal, not residual
🔴 Critical
Yen’s local independence is the residual correlation, but the study sets the marginal one; the LI baseline marginal depends on the unit (≈ 0.79 per total score, ≈ 0.43 per item). 0.2 sits below both (negative residual — not a violation), 0.9 above both (genuine violation), and 0.6 between them — a genuine positive violation under the dissertation’s “among items” reading
Report the realized residual correlation per condition; re-manipulate on the residual scale; re-scope the “robust” claim


7
Additional concerns (count, ANOVA hygiene, review coding)
🟡 Moderate · housekeeping
Condition count 81 vs. stated 108 (+ a stray n = 900); per-indicator ANOVAs ignore clustering and lean on the η² ≥ .01 bar; the systematic review is single-coded (additive, not fatal)
Reconcile the grid + release the log; replicate-level / mixed-effects analysis; double-code ~20% and report κ






How to read the severity column (most pressing first). 🔴 Critical — fatal to the specific claim unless addressed; Chapters 1 and 2 are the two hardest to defend (“must-ask”). 🟠 Fundamental — undermines the central construct of both studies (Chapter 5 reframes everything). 🟡 Moderate — additive housekeeping a journal will expect, but not a threat to the core argument. One caution on the colours: they track how urgently a specific reviewer question lands, not overall depth — so 🟠 Fundamental is not a milder 🔴. It is the one construct-level issue, and the highest-leverage thing to fix first in the Revision Roadmap (Chapter 5), even though the 🔴 items are the ones a reviewer raises first.
Where to start — two orders, on purpose. These notes run in one order but point to another for revision, so it helps to name both up front. For reading, the chapters come hardest-to-defend first — so Chapter 1 (ψ) and Chapter 2 (the location collapse) lead, the questions a reviewer is most likely to raise first, and the two hardest to explain from the outside. For revision, the order is different: the Revision Roadmap at the end runs highest-leverage first, and its Tier 1 opens with Chapter 5, because once “sensitivity” means detection and is measured as detection, every other concern becomes easier to state and to answer. The two orders differ for a simple reason: reading hardest-first surfaces the sharpest questions, while revising leverage-first lets each fix clear the ground for the next. Every chapter ends with the exact question to be ready for.

title: 1  The HRM’s ψ cannot be estimated for misfit raters
text:
1.1 What the dissertation does
The Hierarchical Rater Model (HRM) analysis is one of the most technically ambitious parts of the dissertation, and the core idea behind it is sound. This chapter examines a single parameter, the rater-variability parameter ψ, because a subtle estimation problem is hiding in the two “misfit” raters. A journal reviewer is likely to find it, and the question is better answered before it is asked.
The HRM works in two stages. Stage 1 turns a student’s latent ability into an ideal rating ξ — the integer category (0–4) the response really deserves. Stage 2 is a signal-detection model (SDM): given that ideal rating, it describes the category a particular rater actually assigns. Equation (6), p. 53, states it as
\[
P(X = k \mid \xi) \;\propto\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{\bigl(k - (\xi + \phi_r)\bigr)^2}{2\,\psi_r^2}\right),
\]
a discretized Gaussian centered on the rater’s ideal-plus-bias score \(\xi + \phi_r\), with spread \(\psi_r\). A small \(\psi_r\) means the rater’s ratings cluster tightly around the ideal; the rater is consistent. As the dissertation puts it, “psi (\(\psi_r\)) represents a rater’s variability, which indicates how consistent the rater is … smaller values are interpreted as higher-quality ratings” (p. 53) — “the rater variability parameter (psi)” (p. 61). That is an entirely sensible reading, for raters who are actually reading the work in front of them.
The dissertation then flags two raters whose ψ is far larger than everyone else’s — about 16.65 and 31.85 (SD 13.31) in Tables 16–17 (p. 65) — and reads these large values as the HRM being sensitive to rater misfit. The intuition is sound. The trouble is what the likelihood surface actually looks like for a rater whose ratings carry no signal.
crumbs: 1  The HRM's ψ cannot be estimated for misfit raters

title: 1  The HRM’s ψ cannot be estimated for misfit raters
text:
1.2 Why this threatens the conclusion
It helps to sit with the intuition before the formalism. Imagine a rater who is not reading the essays at all — someone assigning scores by rolling a five-sided die. Their ratings are pure noise, equally likely to land on any category, with no relation to ξ. Now ask what Equation (6) does for that rater. As \(\psi_r\) grows, the Gaussian in the exponent flattens; by \(\psi_r \approx 5\) the five category probabilities are already nearly equal, and as \(\psi_r\) keeps growing the distribution becomes exactly uniform — every category at 0.20, for any ideal rating. In the model’s own language, a rater who chooses at random is a rater with \(\psi_r \to \infty\). Panel A of Figure 1.1 shows this collapse.
That is the heart of the problem. Identification means the data can, in principle, pin down a parameter — that there is a unique value that fits best. The tool for finding it is the profile log-likelihood: the log-likelihood as a function of \(\psi_r\), maximized over everything else. For a well-behaved parameter this curve rises to a clear peak at the best-fitting value (the maximum-likelihood estimate, MLE), then falls away. (Picture a topographic map: we are hunting for the single hilltop — a well-identified parameter has one; a non-identified one is an endless flat ridge with no summit.) For a genuine rater, a stack of essays gives the model real leverage, and the curve peaks cleanly.
For a misfit rater whose ratings are uniform noise, the model cannot tell \(\psi_r = 16\) from \(\psi_r = 32\) from \(\psi_r = 1{,}000\): all of them produce the same near-uniform distribution, so all of them fit equally well. The profile log-likelihood does not peak — it rises and then flattens into a plateau, never turning over into a peak. There is no finite MLE. Panel B of Figure 1.1 shows exactly this. These notes implement Equation (6) from scratch in R — not a re-run of immer, but an independent coding of the dissertation’s own model, done as a diligence check — and plot the profile log-likelihood for two raters. A consistent rater (true \(\psi \approx 0.5\)) shows a sharp peak. A noise rater shows a flat plateau with no peak anywhere out to ψ = 60. The shaded band marks the reported range \(\psi \in [16, 32]\) — and it sits squarely on the flat part of the noise rater’s curve.


Show the R code that builds this figure (implements Eq. 6 from scratch)
source("R/common.R")                       # the dissertation's own equations, in R
library(ggplot2); library(patchwork)

# ---- one consistent rater and one pure-noise ("misfit") rater ----------------
set.seed(11)
N     <- 500L
theta <- rnorm(N)
xi    <- max.col(gpcm_probs(theta)) - 1L                 # ideal rating 0..4
X_consistent <- simulate_rating(sdm_probs(xi, phi = 0, psi = 0.5))  # true ψ = 0.5
X_misfit     <- sample(0:4, N, replace = TRUE)                       # uniform noise

# ---- profile log-likelihood of ψ for each rater ------------------------------
psi_grid <- seq(0.2, 60, length.out = 240)
ll_cons  <- sapply(psi_grid, function(p) sdm_loglik(X_consistent, xi, 0, p))
ll_mis   <- sapply(psi_grid, function(p) sdm_loglik(X_misfit,     xi, 0, p))

# ---- Panel A: the SDM flattens to uniform as ψ grows -------------------------
psi_vals <- c(0.4, 1, 2, 5, 20)
panA <- do.call(rbind, lapply(psi_vals, function(pv) data.frame(
  k = CATS, prob = as.numeric(sdm_probs(2, 0, pv)),
  psi = factor(sprintf("ψ = %g", pv), levels = sprintf("ψ = %g", psi_vals)))))
ramp <- c("#1A5276", "#2471A3", "#85C1E9", "#B9770E", "#C0392B")
names(ramp) <- sprintf("ψ = %g", psi_vals)

pA <- ggplot(panA, aes(k, prob, colour = psi, group = psi)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1/5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey55", linewidth = .5) +
  annotate("text", x = 4, y = 1/5 + .03, label = "uniform 1/5",
           colour = "grey45", size = 3, hjust = 1) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.05) + geom_point(size = 2.1) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = ramp, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:4, labels = c("0", "1", "2\n(= ideal ξ)", "3", "4")) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, .25),
                     labels = scales::percent_format(1)) +
  labs(title = "A  ·  As ψ grows, every score becomes equally likely",
       x = "Observed rating  k", y = "P(X = k | ξ = 2)") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(legend.position = "right")

# ---- Panel B: profile log-likelihood on a log-ψ axis ------------------------
panB <- rbind(
  data.frame(psi = psi_grid, ll = ll_cons - max(ll_cons), rater = "A real rater (true ψ = 0.5)"),
  data.frame(psi = psi_grid, ll = ll_mis  - max(ll_mis),  rater = "A noise rater (the 'misfit')"))
panB$rater <- factor(panB$rater, levels = c("A real rater (true ψ = 0.5)", "A noise rater (the 'misfit')"))

pB <- ggplot(panB, aes(psi, ll, colour = rater)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 16, xmax = 32, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = pal_companion[["warn"]], alpha = .15) +
  annotate("text", x = sqrt(16 * 32), y = -1.2, label = "dissertation's\nreported ψ (16–32)",
           colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], size = 2.9, fontface = "bold", lineheight = .9) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.15) +
  annotate("text", x = 6.5, y = -7.5, hjust = 0,
           label = "the noise rater's curve flattens\nto a plateau — no best ψ exists",
           colour = pal_companion[["misfit"]], size = 3, fontface = "italic", lineheight = .9) +
  annotate("point", x = 0.5, y = 0, colour = pal_companion[["none"]], size = 2.4) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("A real rater (true ψ = 0.5)" = pal_companion[["none"]],
                                 "A noise rater (the 'misfit')" = pal_companion[["misfit"]]), name = NULL) +
  scale_x_log10(breaks = c(0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 16, 32, 60)) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-15, 1)) +
  labs(title = "B  ·  A real rater's ψ has a clear best value; a noise rater's does not",
       x = "ψ  (log scale)", y = "profile log-likelihood\n(each curve − its own max)") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(legend.position = "right", panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank())

pA / pB








Figure 1.1: A real rater’s ψ has a clear best value; a noise rater’s does not. (A) As ψ grows, the signal-detection model flattens to a uniform distribution (every category at 1/5) — so a rater’s ψ can only be pinned down if their ratings actually carry signal. (B) On a log-ψ axis, a consistent rater’s profile log-likelihood peaks sharply near its true ψ ≈ 0.5, but a pure-noise (“misfit”) rater’s rises and then flattens to a plateau: there is no finite maximum. The dissertation’s reported ψ ≈ 16–32 (shaded) lies on that flat plateau.





So \(\psi = 16\) and \(\psi = 32\) are not two different variability estimates; they are two places the sampler happened to pause while wandering across a flat ridge. The number carries essentially no information about the rater. One might hope the prior would rescue things — but the prior is \(\psi_r \sim N(0.4, 10^2)\) (p. 61), with a standard deviation of 10. This prior is weak, and over small \(\psi\) it is indeed fairly flat — at \(\psi = 5\) its density is still about 90% of the peak at \(\psi = 0.4\), and at \(\psi = 10\) about 63%. But over the reported range it is not flat at all: relative to that peak, the density falls to roughly 30% at \(\psi = 16\) and to about 0.7% at \(\psi = 32\), all but vanishing by \(\psi = 60\). So the prior is not propping these values up — if anything it leans the other way, pulling toward small \(\psi\) and penalizing exactly the large values that were reported. That makes a posterior still settling at \(\psi = 16\)–\(32\) more telling, not less: those numbers survive against the prior’s gentle downward pull, which means it is the unconstrained likelihood ridge — not any help from the prior — that places them out there. (It is also worth noting that a normal prior on a quantity that must be positive is itself a little awkward.) And a plain sanity check seals it: \(\psi = 30\) sets the Gaussian spread parameter in Equation (6) to 30 — seven times the width of the whole 0–4 scale — so the model is essentially uniform whatever the ideal rating. At that point ψ has stopped measuring “consistency” at all: 16 and 30 are not two amounts of variability, just two points on the same flat likelihood ridge.
This matters downstream. Tables 16–17 average ψ across raters and feed it into an ANOVA. Averaging a non-identified quantity yields a mean that inherits the same indeterminacy — so the ANOVA’s “sensitivity” result for ψ rests on numbers that do not mean what the label says.






NoteThe strongest counterargument



The large ψ for those two raters is not a malfunction — it is the HRM working exactly as intended. A rater whose ratings carry no signal genuinely does have unbounded variability, so a ψ that runs out to 16, 32, and beyond is the model faithfully reporting that fact; flagging such raters as extreme is precisely what a sensitivity analysis is for. The dissertation did not paper over the instability — it noted the related \(a_i\) convergence caveat.
And yet — that is the right intuition pointed at the wrong number, and the flag is the part worth keeping. The narrow thing the revision asks is bookkeeping: a quantity the profile likelihood cannot bound (Panel B of Figure 1.1) should be reported as flagged and non-estimable, not entered as a point estimate carrying an SD of 13.31 and then averaged into an ANOVA as though it were measured. “The HRM detected that this rater’s variability is unbounded” and “this rater’s ψ is 16.65” are different claims; only the first is one the data support. Reporting ψ as non-identified is not a retreat from the result — it is the result, in the one form a reviewer cannot dispute.

title: 1  The HRM’s ψ cannot be estimated for misfit raters
text:
1.3 What would fix it
The good news: none of this requires new data or abandoning the HRM. It asks for a more careful accounting of what the model can and cannot say about those two raters — and, reframed, the misfit finding actually becomes more interesting.






TipFix-it




Treat the misfit ψ as non-estimable. For a rater whose ratings are consistent with uniform noise (Chapter 3 gives a formal criterion), report ψ as non-identified rather than as a point estimate with an SD. This is a substantive finding, not a weakness: the HRM has detected that the rater’s variability is unbounded given the data.
Reparameterize on the log scale with a regularizing prior. Work with \(\log\psi_r\) — it keeps ψ positive and tames the tail. Something like \(\log\psi_r \sim N(0, 1)\) (so ψ is a priori roughly 0.1–10) regularizes meaningfully without being dogmatic, and barely moves the well-identified raters.
Report diagnostics that separate two questions: is the sampler exploring well (mixing), and can the data pin ψ down at all (identification)? They are not the same, and they are easy to conflate. For mixing, give each rater’s ψ an effective sample size (ESS) and Monte-Carlo standard error (MCSE) — but read them for what they measure: ESS is roughly the retained draws over the autocorrelation time (\(\text{ESS} \approx N/\tau\)), so it simply grows as the chain runs longer and says nothing, by itself, about whether ψ is identified. For identification, lean on the checks this chapter already uses: the profile log-likelihood (a clean peak means the data fix a best ψ; a flat ridge with no finite maximum — Panel B of Figure 1.1 — means they cannot), whether the posterior is essentially just the prior reshaped, posterior mass that drifts toward ever-larger ψ instead of concentrating, and a prior-sensitivity refit (a tighter and a looser prior — a posterior that moves when only the prior changes is prior-driven, not data-driven). The two genuinely come apart: a sampler can glide along the flat ψ ridge with perfectly healthy ESS and still be unable to resolve ψ — which is exactly why the likelihood-side checks are the ones that settle it.
Add a posterior-predictive check for the misfit raters — and read its non-failure as the signal. Draw replicated rating sets from the fitted HRM posterior for those two raters and overlay their category frequencies on the observed ratings (a bar-style PPC, or a Bayesian p-value on the category counts). The point is the reverse of a usual check: the model will pass, and pass for every ψ across the reported 16–32 range and well beyond, because once ψ is large the SDM reproduces the near-uniform observed pattern no matter its exact value. A check the data cannot fail — flat across a wide ψ band — is the in-sample, posterior-side counterpart of the flat profile likelihood in Panel B of Figure 1.1: it confirms the ratings simply do not distinguish one large ψ from another.
Report a bounded, estimable quantity for the misfit raters in place of an averaged ψ. The mean of a non-identified ψ is not worth tabulating; a classification result is. Give the rate at which the HRM correctly flags the misfit raters as misfitting — a detection/flag rate with its uncertainty (a Wilson or posterior interval over replications) — and/or, per rater, the posterior probability that ψ exceeds a stated “noise” threshold, \(\Pr(\psi_r > \psi^*)\) for a pre-specified cut (Chapter 3’s criterion gives a principled one). These are estimable precisely where the mean of ψ is not: \(\Pr(\psi_r > \psi^*)\) stays near 1 whether the true value is 16 or 1,000, so it is stable along the very ridge that makes the point estimate meaningless. This is the concrete, reportable form of “the HRM flags these raters, it does not measure them.”
Limit ψ claims to identified conditions. Restrict the ANOVA and any “sensitivity” language to raters whose ψ has a finite peak; discuss the misfit raters as cases the HRM flags, not cases it measures.
crumbs: 1  The HRM's ψ cannot be estimated for misfit raters

title: 1  The HRM’s ψ cannot be estimated for misfit raters
text:
1.4 What a reviewer will ask






WarningWhat a reviewer will ask



“In what sense is a reported ψ of 16–32 an estimate, given that the profile likelihood for these raters has no finite maximum? And what justifies averaging it across raters and entering it into an ANOVA?”


This question is precise, and it is answerable — but only after the revisions above. Done well, the fix turns a vulnerability into a demonstration of methodological care: the diagnostics are run, ψ is found to be non-identified for those two raters, and it is reported accordingly. That is what careful Bayesian practice looks like, and it makes the misfit result stronger, not weaker — the HRM correctly flags raters whose variability the data cannot bound.

title: 2  The vanishing severe rater: a location estimate that collapses with sample size
text:
2.1 What the dissertation does
This chapter concerns one of the most puzzling numbers in the dissertation — a result that contradicts what a working estimator should do and that resists explanation from the outside. The thread, once pulled, leads somewhere instructive and would make the journal manuscript markedly stronger. The numbers are in Table 14 (p. 64); the design is on pp. 55–56.
A rater’s location in the Many-Facet Rasch Model (MFRM) is a number on the logit scale that says how much harsher or easier that rater is than the group: a positive location marks a severe rater (one who tends to assign lower scores). In the condition where some raters are generated as genuinely severe — their true generating mean is \(\lambda = 2.0\) — the estimated severe-rater location, in the complete design, reads:
The dissertation reads this as severe raters being most distinctly identified when the sample is small. The design is transparent: rater count scales at one rater per ten examinees (p. 55), so there are 5, 10, and 50 raters at the three sample sizes; 10% of them are severe effect raters (p. 56) — which works out to 1, 1, and 5 effect raters. Several SD cells are blank because some conditions “include only 1 or 2 raters” (p. 64). And the normal raters in this same severe condition come out positive (+0.40, +0.07, +0.03) where a mean-centered scale predicts the opposite sign — and at N = 50 the discrepancy is exact. With one severe rater (\(\lambda = 2\)) among five, mean-centering puts the grand offset at \(2/5 = +0.40\), so a centered scale predicts the severe rater at \(2 - 0.40 = +1.60\) (the reported 1.61, essentially on the nose) and the four normal raters at \(0 - 0.40 = -0.40\). They are instead reported at +0.40 — the predicted magnitude with its sign reversed, not merely a little high; so the five locations average \((1.61 + 4 \times 0.40)/5 \approx +0.64\) rather than the \(\approx 0\) a mean-centered scale requires. That clean reversal — not the overall drift — is the sharp end of the thread, pointing to the centering/sign step (a sign convention, an anchoring choice, or a condition-labeling step) behaving unlike a mean-centered MFRM, to be pinned down by a look at the code. Hold that whole picture in mind: the severe group drifts toward zero as N grows, the normal group also drifts, and at the two smallest N the “group mean” is one rater’s estimate.

title: 2  The vanishing severe rater: a location estimate that collapses with sample size
text:
2.2 Why this threatens the conclusion
The numbers move in the wrong direction. The true severity is \(\lambda \approx 2.0\). A working estimator, given more data, should pull the estimate toward the truth and hold it there — that is essentially the definition of a consistent estimator. Instead the estimate is closest to the truth at N = 50 (1.61) and has collapsed to 0.26 — about one-seventh of the true value — by N = 500. More information has made the answer worse.
The most likely culprit is the centering (identification) constraint. Because MFRM locations are defined only relative to one another, the software must fix their mean (or sum) to zero before it can report individual values. So a reported “severe” location is not absolute severity; it is severity relative to the average of whatever raters are in that run. At N = 50 there is 1 severe rater among 5; at N = 500 there are 5 among 50. If the non-severe raters’ estimates also drift — and their reported positive sign, where a mean-centered scale predicts a negative one (−0.40 at N = 50), says they do — then the zero point itself is moving from condition to condition, and the reported collapse is partly a measurement of that moving reference point rather than of the raters.
There is also a confound sitting underneath the word “sample size.” Increasing N from 50 to 500 changes four things at once: the precision of each estimate (the Fisher standard error falls from about 0.072 to 0.051 to 0.023), the number of raters (5 → 10 → 50), the number of severe raters (1 → 1 → 5), and the number of ratings each rater gives (250 → 500 → 2,500). At N = 50 and N = 100 the “severe location mean” is therefore one rater’s estimate, and “sample size” is not a clean experimental lever at all.
To see whether the collapse is a property of the model or of the pipeline, an independent marginal-ML MFRM was implemented in R, with the true ability distribution and item parameters held known — this is not a re-run of TAM, but a separate implementation built only to check recovery against ground truth. It recovers a stable severe location of roughly 1.5–2.0 at every sample size (about 1.50, 2.03, and 1.85). It does exactly what more data should do: it holds the estimate steady and tightens the uncertainty. Figure 2.1 puts the two side by side.


Show the R code (independent marginal-ML MFRM recovery)
suppressPackageStartupMessages({ library(ggplot2); library(patchwork); library(statmod) })
source("R/common.R")

set.seed(99)
I <- 5L; Ns <- c(50L, 100L, 500L); delta_true <- rnorm(I, 0, 0.5)

# average model score variance W, and Fisher SE of a rater location
W       <- mean(vapply(seq_len(I), function(i)
             mean(score_variance(pcmfrm_probs(rnorm(40000), 0, delta_true[i], alpha = 1))), numeric(1)))
se_by_N <- 1 / sqrt(Ns * I * W)
n_eff   <- pmax(1L, round(0.1 * (Ns %/% 10L)))          # 1, 1, 5
rpr     <- I * Ns                                        # 250, 500, 2500

# ---- independent marginal-ML recovery (Gauss–Hermite over theta ~ N(0,1)) ----
gh <- statmod::gauss.quad(41L, "hermite"); theta_q <- sqrt(2) * gh$nodes; wq <- gh$weights / sqrt(pi)
lam_grid <- seq(-1, 4, by = 0.05); G <- length(lam_grid)
marg <- array(0, c(G, I, K))
for (g in seq_len(G)) for (i in seq_len(I))
  marg[g, i, ] <- colSums(pcmfrm_probs(theta_q, lam_grid[g], delta_true[i], alpha = 1) * wq)
log_marg <- log(pmax(marg, 1e-300))

estimate_lambda <- function(counts) {                    # grid argmax + parabolic refine
  ll <- vapply(seq_len(G), function(g) sum(counts * log_marg[g, , ]), numeric(1))
  g <- which.max(ll)
  if (g > 1L && g < G) {
    h <- lam_grid[2] - lam_grid[1]; d <- ll[g-1] - 2*ll[g] + ll[g+1]
    if (abs(d) > 1e-12) return(lam_grid[g] + max(-h, min(h, -0.5*h*(ll[g+1]-ll[g-1])/d)))
  }
  lam_grid[g]
}
recover <- function(N, n_rep) {                          # mean centered severe location
  R <- N %/% 10L; ne <- pmax(1L, round(0.1 * R))
  vapply(seq_len(n_rep), function(.) {
    lam <- rnorm(R, 0, 0.5); eff <- sample(R, ne); lam[eff] <- rnorm(ne, 2, 0.5)
    th  <- rnorm(N)
    lam_hat <- vapply(seq_len(R), function(r) {
      counts <- matrix(0L, I, K)
      for (i in seq_len(I)) { x <- simulate_rating(pcmfrm_probs(th, lam[r], delta_true[i], alpha = 1))
        for (k in seq_len(K)) counts[i, k] <- sum(x == (k - 1L)) }
      estimate_lambda(counts)
    }, numeric(1))
    mean((lam_hat - mean(lam_hat))[eff])
  }, numeric(1))
}
n_rep_by_N <- c(50L, 25L, 8L)
rec <- lapply(seq_along(Ns), function(j) { set.seed(100L + j); recover(Ns[j], n_rep_by_N[j]) })
rec_m <- vapply(rec, mean, numeric(1)); rec_s <- vapply(rec, sd, numeric(1))

diss_sev <- c(1.61, 0.62, 0.26); diss_norm <- c(0.40, 0.07, 0.03)
col_rec <- "#117A65"; col_sev <- "#C0392B"; col_norm <- "#4C4C4C"

# ---- Panel A: the confound ---------------------------------------------------
dfA <- data.frame(N = factor(Ns), se = se_by_N,
                  ann = sprintf("Raters: %d\nEffect: %d\nRatings: %s", Ns %/% 10L, n_eff,
                                formatC(rpr, format = "d", big.mark = ",")))
pA <- ggplot(dfA, aes(N, se, fill = N)) +
  geom_col(width = .55, colour = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("SE = %.3f", se), y = se + .0015), vjust = 0, size = 3.2,
            fontface = "bold", colour = "grey20") +
  geom_text(aes(label = ann, y = se * .42), size = 2.65, lineheight = 1.3, colour = "white") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#2471A3", "#117A65", "#B9770E"), guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, .097), breaks = seq(0, .08, .02),
                     labels = c("0", ".02", ".04", ".06", ".08"), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(title = "(A)  Increasing N changes four things at once",
       subtitle = "Every bar is a different design — not just a larger sample",
       x = "Sample size N", y = "Fisher SE of rater location") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9),
                              panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())

# ---- Panel B: stable recovery vs reported collapse --------------------------
dfB <- data.frame(N = Ns, m = rec_m, lo = rec_m - rec_s, hi = rec_m + rec_s)
pB <- ggplot() +
  geom_ribbon(data = dfB, aes(N, ymin = lo, ymax = hi), fill = col_rec, alpha = .18) +
  geom_line(data = dfB, aes(N, m, colour = "Independent recovery"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(data = dfB, aes(N, m, colour = "Independent recovery"), size = 3) +
  geom_line(data = data.frame(N = Ns, m = diss_sev), aes(N, m, colour = "Reported: severe"),
            linewidth = 1.1, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(data = data.frame(N = Ns, m = diss_sev), aes(N, m, colour = "Reported: severe"),
             size = 3, shape = 17) +
  geom_line(data = data.frame(N = Ns, m = diss_norm), aes(N, m, colour = "Reported: normal"),
            linewidth = .85, linetype = "dotted") +
  geom_point(data = data.frame(N = Ns, m = diss_norm), aes(N, m, colour = "Reported: normal"),
             size = 2.5, shape = 15) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey70", linewidth = .4) +
  annotate("text", x = 155, y = 1.20, lineheight = .95, colour = col_sev, size = 2.85,
           fontface = "italic", label = "more data,\nyet the signal\ndisappears (?)") +
  annotate("segment", x = 230, xend = 430, y = .92, yend = .34, colour = col_sev, linewidth = .4,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(.16, "cm"), type = "closed")) +
  scale_colour_manual(name = NULL,
    values = c("Independent recovery" = col_rec, "Reported: severe" = col_sev, "Reported: normal" = col_norm),
    breaks = c("Independent recovery", "Reported: severe", "Reported: normal"),
    guide = guide_legend(override.aes = list(linetype = c("solid","dashed","dotted"), shape = c(16,17,15)))) +
  scale_x_log10(breaks = Ns, labels = Ns) + scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-.1, 2.6), breaks = seq(0, 2.5, .5)) +
  labs(title = "(B)  The collapse points to the pipeline, not the model",
       subtitle = "Independent recovery (teal) is stable; reported values (red) collapse",
       x = "Sample size N", y = "Severe-rater location estimate") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9),
                              legend.text = element_text(size = 8), legend.key.width = unit(1.0, "lines"))

pA | pB








Figure 2.1: The collapse is not implied by the model — it points to the pipeline. (A) Increasing N changes four things at once — the standard error, the number of raters, the number of severe raters (1/1/5), and the ratings per rater — so “sample size” is not a clean factor. (B) On a log-N axis, an independent marginal-ML MFRM (teal) recovers a stable severe-rater location near 1.5–2.0 at every N, while the dissertation’s reported value (red) collapses 1.61 → 0.62 → 0.26 toward the normal raters (grey). A consistent estimator does the opposite of collapsing.





So the most plausible reading is that the collapse comes from the centering, anchoring, or condition-labeling step — a reference point that shifts with N — rather than from the MFRM. This is not a flaw in the core argument; it is a pipeline detail that, once found, will very likely restore the stable recovery the theory predicts.

title: 2  The vanishing severe rater: a location estimate that collapses with sample size
text:
2.3 What would fix it






TipFix-it




Report recovery as a per-condition table, not just estimates. Build one recovery table with a row per condition × rater type (severe vs. normal) and named columns: true severity \(\lambda\), raw (uncentered) estimate, centered estimate, bias (centered − true), RMSE, coverage, Monte-Carlo SE, and convergence status. Pair it with the true-vs-estimated \(\lambda\) scatterplot faceted by condition, and keep the rank correlation between estimated and true severity across raters. Named columns make the diagnosis answerable at a glance: raw beside centered shows whether the centering step is what moves (the +0.40 sign reversal jumps out there), bias beside RMSE separates a systematic shift from noise, coverage flags intervals that are too narrow, and Monte-Carlo SE with convergence status guards against reading a sampler artifact — especially in the single-effect-rater cells — as a finding.
Untangle the confound. Replace the single “sample size” factor with a grid that separates examinee N, rater count, effect-rater count, and ratings per rater. Even a post-hoc table labeling each existing condition by all four clarifies what was actually manipulated.
Audit the centering and its sign, condition by condition. Print the sum (and mean) of estimated rater locations per replication. At N = 50 the tell is sharp: a mean-centered solution forces the normal raters to \(-0.40\) and the five-rater mean to \(0\), yet they are reported at \(+0.40\) with the locations averaging \(\approx +0.64\) — the predicted normal value with its sign reversed. Check the sign convention on the centering/anchoring call, not just whether a constraint is applied; the positive normal-rater values (+0.40 / 0.07 / 0.03) are the thread to pull.
Add a sensitivity analysis for the single-effect-rater cells (N = 50, 100), where the “severe mean” is one estimate, and flag those cells explicitly.
Share the simulation code and condition logs. One look at the call that produces the N = 500 results will most likely make the source of the collapse visible.

title: 2  The vanishing severe rater: a location estimate that collapses with sample size
text:
2.4 What a reviewer will ask






WarningWhat a reviewer will ask



“Why does the severe-rater location decline from about 1.61 to 0.26 as the sample grows, when more information should stabilize it toward the true value? Please show the recovery of the rater locations against their true generating values, condition by condition, and confirm the centering constraint is applied consistently across sample sizes.”


The number is strange precisely because it contradicts what MFRM estimation should do. Strange numbers, followed carefully, almost always lead somewhere worth knowing: a line in the pipeline, a condition label, a centering call that behaves differently at 5 raters than at 50. Tracking it down would yield a thorough understanding of the internals of this simulation — the footing the journal version requires.

title: 3  The ‘misfit’ rater is a coin flip
text:
3.1 What the dissertation does
Chapter 3 is one of the more technically ambitious parts of the study: it builds inaccuracy directly into the data-generating model rather than gesturing at it. This chapter concerns a single modeling choice inside that design that, once adjusted, strengthens the journal version. The relevant pages are 55–58.
To create a “generally inaccurate” or misfitting rater, the simulation adds a rater slope (discrimination) parameter \(\alpha\) to the data-generating MFRM (Equation 7, introduced p. 55). Normal raters get \(\alpha = 1\) — the standard Rasch assumption. Inaccurate raters take a decisive step: “we manipulated the slope parameter by setting its mean to 0, whereas the slope parameter for the normal raters was fixed at 1” (p. 57). The rationale is that “when raters have lower slopes, they exhibit less discrimination between examinees” (p. 57), and this third condition is labeled “general inaccuracy (i.e., misfit or haphazard ratings)” (p. 57). On the HRM side, “rater misfit can be broadly interpreted as rater variability and is reflected in the psi parameter” (p. 58).
The intuition is sound — a rater who cannot tell high-ability from low-ability examinees is, in a real sense, inaccurate. The question is how far the design takes that intuition: all the way to \(\alpha = 0\).
crumbs: 3  The 'misfit' rater is a coin flip

title: 3  The ‘misfit’ rater is a coin flip
text:
3.2 Why this threatens the conclusion
Consider what a slope of exactly zero does, worked out from Equation 7. In the adjacent-category model, the log-odds of category \(k\) over category \(k-1\) is \(\alpha\) times the usual Rasch term. Set \(\alpha = 0\) and every one of those log-odds collapses to zero at once — which forces all five categories to probability exactly \(0.20\). The distribution is perfectly uniform, independent of the examinee’s ability, the item, and even the rater’s own severity \(\lambda\) (because \(\lambda\) is multiplied by \(\alpha\), and anything times zero is zero). The rater is, quite literally, rolling a five-sided die.
One point is worth stating plainly, because the design is a little more careful than every misfit rater being a die. The slope mean is set to 0 — the misfit slopes are drawn from a distribution centered at zero (almost certainly \(\alpha \sim N(0, 0.05)\), mirroring the normal raters’ \(N(1, 0.05)\)), not pinned there. So \(\alpha = 0\) is the expected, typical misfit rater — the case the algebra above describes — and individual misfit raters scatter around it: at the study’s sample sizes their realized score-to-ability correlations spread by roughly \(\pm 0.4\) (largely finite-sample noise plus the small slope spread), with a mean near zero. That refinement does not soften the point — it sharpens it. Because the mean slope is 0, the condition is centered on the no-signal case: on average these raters carry no ability signal, so the inflated fit is still the expected, mechanical consequence — a positive control, not evidence of sensitivity to realistic inaccuracy. The accurate claim is that the typical misfit rater is a coin flip, not that every one is exactly uniform.
Equation 7 was re-implemented from scratch in R — not a re-run of TAM, but a ground-up reconstruction — and confirmed. Figure 3.1 makes it concrete. Panel A puts the two raters side by side: the accurate rater (\(\alpha = 1\)) produces a peaked, ability-tracking distribution with a score-to-ability correlation of about 0.88; the “inaccurate” rater (\(\alpha = 0\)) produces five equal bars at \(0.20\), with a correlation of about 0.00. These are not two points on a quality continuum — they are qualitatively different things. Panel B sweeps the slope from 1 down to 0 and shows the MFRM infit/outfit rising smoothly from \(\approx 1.0\) to \(\approx 3.5\)–\(4.3\), passing straight through the dissertation’s reported misfit band (\(\approx 2.0\)–\(3.2\)). The inflated fit is a mechanical consequence of the slope knob, not a discovery.


Show the R code (implements the dissertation’s Eq. 7 from scratch)
suppressPackageStartupMessages({ library(ggplot2); library(patchwork) })
source("R/common.R")

set.seed(20260609)
N_EX <- 500L; I <- 5L; delta <- rnorm(I, 0, 0.5)

one_rater_observed <- function(theta, alpha, lam = 0) {
  obs <- matrix(0L, length(theta), I)
  for (i in seq_len(I)) obs[, i] <- simulate_rating(pcmfrm_probs(theta, lam, delta[i], alpha = alpha))
  obs
}
oracle_fit <- function(theta, observed) {              # scored vs the slope-1 analysis model
  z2 <- var <- numeric(0)
  for (i in seq_len(I)) {
    p <- pcmfrm_probs(theta, 0, delta[i], alpha = 1); ex <- expected_score(p); v <- pmax(score_variance(p), 1e-9)
    z2 <- c(z2, (observed[, i] - ex)^2 / v); var <- c(var, v)
  }
  list(infit = sum(z2 * var) / sum(var), outfit = mean(z2))
}

# ---- Panel A: accurate vs misfit category frequencies -----------------------
theta_A <- rnorm(N_EX)
obs_norm <- one_rater_observed(theta_A, 1); obs_mis <- one_rater_observed(theta_A, 0)
freq_norm <- tabulate(as.integer(obs_norm) + 1L, K) / length(obs_norm)
freq_mis  <- tabulate(as.integer(obs_mis)  + 1L, K) / length(obs_mis)
r_norm <- cor(rowSums(obs_norm), theta_A); r_mis <- cor(rowSums(obs_mis), theta_A)

dfA <- rbind(data.frame(rater = "Accurate rater (slope = 1)", k = factor(CATS), freq = freq_norm),
             data.frame(rater = "Misfit rater (slope = 0)",  k = factor(CATS), freq = freq_mis))
dfA$rater <- factor(dfA$rater, levels = c("Accurate rater (slope = 1)", "Misfit rater (slope = 0)"))
annA <- data.frame(rater = factor(levels(dfA$rater), levels = levels(dfA$rater)),
                   label = c(sprintf("r = %.2f", r_norm), sprintf("r = %.2f", r_mis)))
pA <- ggplot(dfA, aes(k, freq, fill = rater)) +
  geom_col(width = .7, colour = "white") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = .2, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40", linewidth = .7) +
  annotate("text", x = .65, y = .215, label = "Uniform 1/5", hjust = 0, size = 2.9,
           colour = "grey35", fontface = "italic") +
  geom_text(data = annA, aes(x = 4.45, y = .37, label = label), inherit.aes = FALSE,
            hjust = 1, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold",
            colour = c(unname(pal_companion["accent"]), unname(pal_companion["misfit"]))) +
  facet_wrap(~ rater) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Accurate rater (slope = 1)" = unname(pal_companion["accent"]),
                               "Misfit rater (slope = 0)"  = unname(pal_companion["misfit"])), guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, .45), breaks = seq(0, .4, .1),
                     labels = c("0", ".10", ".20", ".30", ".40"), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(title = "(A)  The 'misfit' rater is a coin-flip",
       subtitle = "slope = 0  →  every category equally likely; no signal about ability",
       x = "Rating category (0–4)", y = "Relative frequency") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9.5),
                              strip.background = element_blank(), panel.spacing = unit(1.2, "lines"),
                              plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9),
                              panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())

# ---- Panel B: fit MSE vs slope ----------------------------------------------
set.seed(20260610)
alphas <- seq(0, 1, .1); n_rep <- 60L
sweep <- do.call(rbind, lapply(alphas, function(a) {
  fr <- replicate(n_rep, { th <- rnorm(N_EX); ob <- one_rater_observed(th, a); f <- oracle_fit(th, ob)
                           c(f$infit, f$outfit, cor(rowSums(ob), th)) })
  data.frame(alpha = a, infit = mean(fr[1, ]), outfit = mean(fr[2, ]), r = mean(fr[3, ]))
}))
dfB <- rbind(data.frame(alpha = sweep$alpha, value = sweep$infit, stat = "Infit MSE"),
             data.frame(alpha = sweep$alpha, value = sweep$outfit, stat = "Outfit MSE"))
pB <- ggplot(dfB, aes(alpha, value, colour = stat)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = -Inf, xmax = Inf, ymin = 2.0, ymax = 3.2, fill = "#C0392B", alpha = .08) +
  annotate("text", x = .82, y = 2.6, label = "dissertation's\nmisfit band\n(2.0–3.2)", hjust = 1,
           size = 2.8, colour = "#C0392B", lineheight = .95, fontface = "italic") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50", linewidth = .6) +
  annotate("text", x = .9, y = 1.28, label = "expected fit = 1.0", hjust = 1, size = 2.9,
           colour = "grey45", fontface = "italic") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  annotate("text", x = .97, y = .55, label = sprintf("r = %.2f", sweep$r[sweep$alpha == 1]),
           hjust = 1, size = 3, colour = "grey30", fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = .03, y = .55, label = sprintf("r = %.2f", sweep$r[sweep$alpha == 0]),
           hjust = 0, size = 3, colour = "grey30", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_colour_manual(name = NULL, values = c("Infit MSE" = unname(pal_companion["misfit"]),
                                              "Outfit MSE" = unname(pal_companion["severe"]))) +
  scale_x_reverse(breaks = seq(0, 1, .2), labels = c("0\n(misfit)", ".2", ".4", ".6", ".8", "1\n(accurate)")) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 6), breaks = 0:6, expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(title = "(B)  'Inaccuracy' is just a signal-to-noise knob",
       subtitle = "MFRM fit rises deterministically as the slope drops — a positive control, not evidence of sensitivity",
       x = "Data-generating slope α (rater discrimination)", y = "Fit MSE (infit / outfit)") +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9),
                              legend.position = "bottom", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

pA | pB








Figure 3.1: A ‘misfit’ rater is a coin flip. (A) With the slope set to 0, the rater’s five categories are essentially equally likely (≈ 0.20 each) and the score-to-ability correlation is ≈ 0 — versus a peaked, ability-tracking profile (r ≈ 0.88) for an accurate rater. (B) Turning the data-generating slope α from 1 (accurate) down to 0 (misfit) makes the MFRM fit mean-square rise smoothly from ≈ 1.0 through the dissertation’s reported misfit band (≈ 2.0–3.2). The inflated fit is a deterministic function of the slope, not a finding.





Three threats follow, and they compound.
Detection is trivialized. A pure-noise rater whose scores carry zero information is not a realistic model of human inaccuracy. Real raters who are inconsistent, fatigued, or idiosyncratic still have some slope — they discriminate imperfectly, not randomly. So the reported detection rates are for an extreme case any reasonable index can flag, not for the realistic inaccuracy a reviewer cares about.
Severity becomes unidentifiable. Because \(\lambda\) is multiplied by zero, the severity of an \(\alpha = 0\) rater has no influence on the data and cannot be estimated. This is the same manipulation that, in Chapter 1, leaves the HRM’s \(\psi\) with no finite value — so Chapters 1 and 3 are two faces of one design choice. Naming that link explicitly strengthens both.
The MFRM has a home-field advantage. Infit and outfit measure deviation from the Rasch model — and the misfit data were made by taking a Rasch/MFRM structure and erasing it (\(\alpha = 0\)). Asking the MFRM to notice that its own structure has been removed is not a neutral test of detection power; it is a model recognizing its own data. A reviewer will see this at once.
And the advantage is not confined to these misfit cells — it runs through all of Study 2. Every condition, None and Severity no less than Misfit, is generated from the same adapted MFRM of Equation 7; the fitted MFRM is that very model with the slope switched off, while the HRM, whose first stage is a GPCM, must recover from a rival model’s data. So the MFRM is always playing at home. The dissertation anticipated exactly this: its Purpose states that “rather than comparing the two models directly, we aim to examine the different patterns each model exhibits under various conditions” (p. 48), and that framing genuinely defuses the concern — a study of how each model behaves on its own terms is not a rigged head-to-head. The catch is that the conclusions still read comparatively in places — which model is more sensitive, and where — and the instant a comparison is drawn, the home-field tilt is back. The clean way to keep the comparative payoff without the tilt is the cross-generative design already in the Fix-it: generate from each model and score with both, so any remaining comparison is symmetric and neither model is graded on its home turf.
There is also an internal tension. The dissertation’s review chapter stresses that fit statistics are non-specific — a high infit/outfit can come from inconsistency, halo, central tendency, range restriction, criterion bias, or model misspecification, not only from haphazard noise (the dissertation says so, p. 58). Yet Chapters 3–4 read a rise in fit directly as “inaccuracy detection.” Under known simulation truth that is fine; but it means the detection claim is conditional on the truth being pure-noise — exactly the specificity the review warns cannot be assumed in practice.






NoteThe strongest counterargument



Setting the misfit slope mean to 0 was not an oversight — it was a deliberate, clean stress test. To verify that the fit indices can detect inaccuracy at all, the disciplined first move is to hand them the most extreme, least ambiguous case and confirm they fire; if an index failed even here, it would fail everywhere. Choosing the limiting case to establish that floor is sound design in the tradition of a positive control, not a belief that real raters roll dice.
And yet — \(\alpha = 0\) is a legitimate positive control, and Panel B’s smooth slope-to-fit curve is genuinely reassuring. What a positive control establishes, though, is only that the index fires in the limit; it cannot, by itself, license the broader claim that the indices are sensitive to the inaccuracy raters show in practice — halo, central tendency, partial mis-discrimination (\(\alpha = 0.2, 0.5,
0.8\)), criterion bias. \(\alpha = 0\) stays as the anchor; the revision adds those realistic mechanisms alongside it and scopes the conclusion to match — “the indices flag a pure-noise rater” is proven now, “the indices detect realistic inaccuracy” needs the realistic cases to earn it.
crumbs: 3  The 'misfit' rater is a coin flip

title: 3  The ‘misfit’ rater is a coin flip
text:
3.3 What would fix it






TipFix-it




Widen the inaccuracy operationalization. Replace the single \(\alpha = 0\) point with partial reductions (\(\alpha = 0.2, 0.5, 0.8\)) and mechanisms drawn from the literature the review synthesizes: threshold/category-usage distortion, central tendency, extremity, criterion- or subgroup-specific bias, and mixture (occasional) noise. These give detection curves over realistic raters, not one extreme point.
Use a neutral, cross-generative design. Generate from a third model that is neither the MFRM nor the HRM (or cross the two symmetrically — generate from each, score with both), so neither model is graded on its home turf and a genuine comparison is possible.
Reconcile with the non-specificity point. Add a Discussion sentence acknowledging that elevated fit is consistent with — not diagnostic of — inaccuracy versus other effects, and frame the rates as “sensitivity to a pure-noise pattern.”
Scope the current result honestly. One sentence: the present finding shows the indices can flag a rater whose scores are informationally empty. That is real and useful — it just needs the right frame.
crumbs: 3  The 'misfit' rater is a coin flip

title: 3  The ‘misfit’ rater is a coin flip
text:
3.4 What a reviewer will ask






WarningWhat a reviewer will ask



“Is a slope of zero — pure uniform noise — a valid operationalization of ‘general inaccuracy’? Human raters who are inattentive or inconsistent still carry some information about examinees. And since infit/outfit measure deviation from the very Rasch model that generated the data, how do you separate the MFRM’s structural advantage from a genuine finding — would the same rates hold under a model-neutral generating process?”


A careful reviewer will not be hostile here — they will likely admire the design and want its bounding conditions. The strongest move is to preempt them: state that \(\alpha = 0\) is an extreme case, show (Panel B) that the slope-to-fit relationship is smooth and continuous, and offer the cross-generative extension. Reviewers reward authors who have already thought the critique through — and the revisions above do exactly that.

title: 4  A single short MCMC chain cannot carry the HRM
text:
4.1 What the dissertation does
The concern in this chapter is narrow: it is about how long the MCMC sampler ran, and what that means for trusting the specific numerical comparisons drawn from it. This discussion appears on pages 60–61.
The HRM is estimated with the R package immer, which — as the dissertation notes — “provides one chain” (p. 60). That single chain runs 1,000 iterations, of which the first 300 are discarded as burn-in (the warm-up phase whose draws are thrown away while the sampler is still finding its way around the posterior). The remaining ~700 draws are the entire posterior sample — the pool from which every estimate, interval, and between-condition comparison is read.
For a sense of scale, the benchmarks cited on the same page: immer’s own default is 500,000 iterations with 200,000 burn-in; Patz et al. (2002) used 10,000 / 1,000; Casabianca et al. used 105,000 / 5,000 (p. 60). The chain is between one-fifteenth and one-hundredth as long as established practice. The priors are diffuse: \(\phi_r \sim N(0, 10^2)\), \(\psi_r \sim N(0.4, 10^2)\), and \(\sigma^2 \sim \text{InverseGamma}(0.001, 1)\) (p. 61).
To check convergence, the dissertation reports a Gelman–Rubin \(\hat{R}\) — a diagnostic that compares between-chain to within-chain variance, with values near 1.0 signaling that chains started from different places have settled on the same distribution. But a real \(\hat{R}\) needs at least two chains, and only one was run; so the reported value is computed “by splitting a single chain into three segments and treat[ing] them as independent chains” (p. 61). Even that weaker substitute flags trouble: “some parameters, such as \(a_i\), exhibited high Rhat values, indicating potential convergence issues” (p. 61). The dissertation concludes the focal results were not strongly affected — but that claim is hard to verify with the evidence at hand.
crumbs: 4  A single short MCMC chain cannot carry the HRM

title: 4  A single short MCMC chain cannot carry the HRM
text:
4.2 Why this threatens the conclusion
The core issue is that 700 draws from one chain is not 700 independent draws. A Markov chain produces autocorrelated samples — each draw resembles the one before it — so it must run long enough for that correlation to decay before it behaves like a random sample. The effective sample size corrects for this: if the integrated autocorrelation time is \(\tau\), then \(\text{ESS} \approx 700/\tau\). For a hierarchical IRT model with hundreds of correlated parameters (an ability for every examinee, an item vector for every item, and every \(\phi_r\) and \(\psi_r\)), estimated by the single-site Metropolis–Hastings sampler immer uses, \(\tau\) is commonly in the tens to hundreds. At \(\tau = 20, 50, 100\), those ~700 retained draws give an ESS of only 35, 14, and 7 — a few dozen genuinely independent draws at best, dwindling to single digits. The remedy is built into that same arithmetic: because \(\text{ESS} \approx N/\tau\), a chain ten times longer would lift these to roughly 350, 140, and 70. The problem is not autocorrelation itself but that 700 draws is too thin a pool to absorb it, and lengthening the chain is exactly what raises the ESS.
That matters because every reported posterior mean carries a Monte-Carlo standard error — the uncertainty from using a finite, autocorrelated sample rather than the true posterior: \(\text{MCSE} = \text{posterior SD} / \sqrt{\text{ESS}}\). With \(\sigma_\phi \approx 0.5\), the MCSE at those ESS values is about 0.085, 0.134, and 0.189 — the same size as the between-condition \(\phi\) differences of roughly 0.05–0.16 interpreted as meaningful. In other words, the Monte-Carlo noise in the estimate is as large as the reported signal. Figure 4.1 shows both halves of this.


Show the R code (illustrative AR(1) surrogate + exact ESS/MCSE)
suppressPackageStartupMessages({ library(ggplot2); library(patchwork) })
source("R/common.R")

set.seed(2026)
N_KEEP <- 700L; SD_PHI <- 0.5; BURN <- 300L; LEN <- 1000L
DIFF_LO <- 0.05; DIFF_HI <- 0.16
ess  <- function(tau) N_KEEP / tau
mcse <- function(tau) SD_PHI / sqrt(ess(tau))
tau_seq <- seq(1, 150, length.out = 400)

ar1 <- function(rho, n, sd = SD_PHI, mu = 0) {          # single-site-MH-like autocorrelation
  x <- numeric(n); x[1] <- rnorm(1, mu, sd); inn <- rnorm(n, 0, sd * sqrt(1 - rho^2))
  for (t in 2:n) x[t] <- mu + rho * (x[t - 1] - mu) + inn[t]; x
}
rho_typ <- (50 - 1) / (50 + 1)                          # τ = 50
chA <- ar1(rho_typ, LEN); chB <- ar1(rho_typ, LEN)
mA <- mean(chA[(BURN + 1):LEN]); mB <- mean(chB[(BURN + 1):LEN])

# Panel A — two single chains disagree
dfA <- rbind(data.frame(it = 1:LEN, phi = chA, chain = "Chain A"),
             data.frame(it = 1:LEN, phi = chB, chain = "Chain B"))
pA <- ggplot(dfA, aes(it, phi, colour = chain)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0, xmax = BURN, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, fill = "grey85", alpha = .6) +
  annotate("text", x = BURN/2, y = 1.7, label = "burn-in\n(300 discarded)", size = 2.7,
           colour = "grey45", lineheight = .9) +
  geom_line(linewidth = .35, alpha = .9) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mA, colour = pal_companion[["misfit"]], linetype = "dashed", linewidth = .8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mB, colour = pal_companion[["severe"]], linetype = "dashed", linewidth = .8) +
  annotate("label", x = 1000, y = 1.92, hjust = 1, vjust = 1, size = 2.55, colour = "grey25",
           fill = "white", alpha = .85, label.size = 0, lineheight = .9,
           label = sprintf("the two post-burn-in means differ by %.2f\n— a single chain cannot reveal this", abs(mA - mB))) +
  scale_colour_manual(name = NULL, values = c("Chain A" = pal_companion[["misfit"]], "Chain B" = pal_companion[["severe"]])) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, 300, 600, 1000)) +
  labs(title = "(A)  Two single 1,000 / 300 chains disagree",
       subtitle = "illustrative AR(1) surrogate — not a re-run of immer", x = "MCMC iteration", y = "φ draw") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(legend.position = "bottom", plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9))

# Panel B — MCSE vs τ, with the interpreted-effect band
dfB <- data.frame(tau = tau_seq, mcse = mcse(tau_seq))
pts <- data.frame(tau = c(20, 50, 100), mcse = mcse(c(20, 50, 100)), ess = ess(c(20, 50, 100)))
pB <- ggplot(dfB, aes(tau, mcse)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = -Inf, xmax = Inf, ymin = DIFF_LO, ymax = DIFF_HI,
           fill = pal_companion[["accent"]], alpha = .15) +
  annotate("text", x = 148, y = 0.088, hjust = 1, vjust = .5, size = 2.85, lineheight = .9,
           colour = pal_companion[["accent"]], fontface = "italic",
           label = "between-condition φ\ndifferences interpreted\n(0.05–0.16)") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1, colour = "#34495E") +
  geom_point(data = pts, aes(tau, mcse), colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], size = 3) +
  geom_text(data = pts, aes(tau, mcse, label = sprintf("ESS = %.0f", ess)),
            vjust = -1.1, size = 3, colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], fontface = "bold") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(1, 20, 50, 100, 150)) + scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 0.25)) +
  labs(title = "(B)  Monte-Carlo error rivals the 'effects'",
       subtitle = "R-hat undefined with one chain;  ESS = 700 / τ,  MCSE = 0.5 / √ESS",
       x = "integrated autocorrelation time  τ  (swept, not estimated)", y = "MCSE of φ posterior mean") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9),
                              panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

pA | pB








Figure 4.1: A single short chain cannot carry the HRM. (A) Two independent chains of the same length (1,000 iterations, 300 burn-in) under single-site-MH-like autocorrelation land at post-burn-in means about 0.14 apart — a gap a single chain cannot reveal. (B) The Monte-Carlo standard error of φ versus the autocorrelation time τ: across the realistic range it lands inside the very band of between-condition differences the study interprets (0.05–0.16); ESS = 700/τ is 35/14/7 at τ = 20/50/100. This is an honest AR(1) surrogate calibrated to the regime — not a re-run of immer; the ESS/MCSE relations are exact algebra.





A note on the figure: it is an illustrative surrogate calibrated to the regime of single-site Metropolis–Hastings on a hierarchical posterior — not a re-run of the immer model, since the data are not available. The ESS and MCSE relationships are exact algebra; the chain-disagreement panel illustrates what is possible in this regime, not a claim about the sampler’s exact \(\tau\). The arithmetic, worked from first principles, reaches the same place.
Two further points. First, the high \(\hat{R}\) for \(a_i\) cannot be safely bracketed: in a hierarchical model \(a_i\), \(\theta\), \(\xi\), \(\phi\), and \(\psi\) are coupled in the joint posterior, so a sampling problem in one is a warning about the whole. The dissertation does not leave this unexamined: it re-checks with longer chains and finds the focal patterns stable (p. 60), and it compares the \(a_i\)-estimated against the \(a_i\)-fixed specification across 20 replications, where the results were consistent (a moderate, positive correlation, with the mean \(\phi\) and \(\psi\) holding up; p. 61). What those checks cannot do is substitute for a convergence statistic: a stable pattern across longer runs, or matching means across replications, is a robustness observation — it tells a reader the result is reproducible — not a formal \(\hat{R}\)/ESS demonstration that the coupled sampler has converged. Second, the diffuse priors compound the issue: \(\text{InverseGamma}(0.001, 1)\) on \(\sigma^2\) is extremely diffuse — its mode sits near 1 and, with shape \(0.001 < 1\), its mean is not even finite, so it spreads enormous prior mass over large \(\sigma^2\) (a very heavy right tail) rather than piling mass near zero. The Limitations section (p. 74) flags that immer’s default inverse-gamma prior “has raised concerns in previous research,” citing Gelman (2006) by name. The one nuance to add is which form Gelman criticized: the pathology is the \(\text{InverseGamma}(\epsilon,
\epsilon)\) case — both parameters tiny — whose pile-up of mass near zero is the genuinely long-criticized behavior, and that is not the \(\text{InverseGamma}(0.001, 1)\) used here. With a scale of 1, this prior behaves the opposite way near the boundary, so Gelman’s specific warning does not land on it. Even so, such a heavy-tailed prior on \(\sigma^2\) can slow mixing, and when convergence is uncertain its influence on \(\phi\) and \(\psi\) cannot be cleanly separated from sampling noise.






NoteThe strongest counterargument



The record shows convergence was not ignored. The high \(\hat{R}\) for some parameters is reported openly in the Limitations (p. 74); additional checks with longer chains found the focal patterns stable (p. 60); the \(a_i\)-estimated specification was compared against the \(a_i\)-fixed specification across 20 replications (p. 61) with consistent results; and the dissertation flags that immer’s default inverse-gamma prior “has raised concerns in previous research,” citing Gelman (2006). This is more convergence diligence than most applied MFRM papers show.
The issue was never awareness; it is the form of the evidence. “The longer chains looked stable” and “the means matched across 20 replications” are robustness observations, not convergence statistics — they tell a reader the result is plausible, not that it is settled. The journal version needs those same checks formalized: a true multi-chain \(\hat{R}\), bulk- and tail-ESS, and an MCSE for each focal \(\phi\) and \(\psi\), so that “the patterns were stable” becomes “ESS = X, MCSE = Y, and each interpreted difference exceeds its MCSE by a comfortable margin.” The judgment-laden part is done; what remains is to report it in the currency the reviewer counts in.
crumbs: 4  A single short MCMC chain cannot carry the HRM

title: 4  A single short MCMC chain cannot carry the HRM
text:
4.3 What would fix it






TipFix-it




Run ≥ 4 independent chains from dispersed starting values — the prerequisite for a meaningful \(\hat{R}\) — each much longer than 1,000/300 (treat Patz’s 10,000/1,000 or Casabianca’s 105,000/5,000 as a floor, and extend if diagnostics warrant). The obstacle is that immer provides only one chain by default, so there are two realistic routes to the four. The immediate, low-cost one: run immer ≥ 4 times from different seeds and dispersed starting values, then pool the runs as chains and compute the diagnostics across them. The more thorough one — and the better long-run investment — is to re-implement the HRM in a native multi-chain sampler (Stan, JAGS, or NIMBLE), which runs the chains jointly and yields proper multi-chain \(\hat{R}\), bulk-/tail-ESS, and MCSE directly.
Report, for every focal parameter (\(\phi_r\), \(\psi_r\)), the true multi-chain \(\hat{R}\), the bulk- and tail-ESS, and the MCSE — and state in the text that each interpreted difference exceeds its MCSE by a comfortable margin.
Show trace plots and per-chain posterior-density overlays for the focal parameters as supplementary material — the most direct evidence a reader can inspect.
Run a prior-sensitivity analysis for \(\phi\) and \(\psi\); replace \(\text{InverseGamma}(0.001,1)\) with a half-Normal or half-Cauchy on the SD scale (which mixes far better), and consider a \(\log(\psi)\) / positivity-constrained parameterization to avoid boundary pathologies.
Add posterior-predictive checks — simulate replicated ratings from the posterior and compare to the observed data — as independent evidence of fit and reach.
crumbs: 4  A single short MCMC chain cannot carry the HRM

title: 4  A single short MCMC chain cannot carry the HRM
text:
4.4 What a reviewer will ask






WarningWhat a reviewer will ask



“What are the effective sample size and Monte-Carlo standard error of \(\phi\) and \(\psi\) at 700 draws from a single chain — and are the between-condition differences you interpret larger than that Monte-Carlo error? And how was an \(\hat{R}\) computed from a single chain?”


immer provides a single chain by default, and a short run is an understandable choice under real time and compute limits — a software-and-resources constraint, not a methodological error. The point is only what the journal version needs to show. Under the current setup the ESS is likely single digits to low tens, the MCSE is of the same order as the reported differences, and the \(\hat{R}\) came from a split-chain approximation that itself flagged problems. The goal of the revision is to answer with specific numbers — ESS = X, MCSE = Y, interpreted difference = Z, and a large Z/Y. This is achievable, and getting there makes the HRM results persuasive rather than merely plausible.
