title: 5  What did we actually measure? Sensitivity vs. a shift in means
text:
5.1 What the dissertation does
This chapter addresses one of the most consequential decisions in both simulation studies — one that is easy to miss precisely because the framing is so intuitive. The mean-shift findings are real and publishable; what needs sharpening is what they are actually measuring. The key definitions are on pp. 48, 62, and 65, the same rule is repeated at p. 86, and the results are in Tables 23–27 (pp. 110–112).
The dissertation defines sensitivity exactly as it should be defined. Page 48 states that “the sensitivity of rater effect indicators refers to the ability of these indices to accurately identify rater effects when they are present” — that is, given that a rater truly has a problem, does the index catch it? This is the detection question, and it is the right one.
To measure it, both studies run an ANOVA over the simulated conditions and report partial η² as the outcome: “we examined partial η² as an effect size, considering values greater than 0.01 as the threshold for practical significance” (p. 62; the same rule in Study 3, p. 86). The analysis is restricted to effect raters — “we focused only on the effect raters” (p. 65) — and the resulting η² values are read as evidence of sensitivity (for example, rater location η² = .51 and HRM ψ η² = .88; Tables 23–27, pp. 110–112).
The logic appears natural: if the index rises when there is a real effect, and the effect of condition on the index is large, the index would seem to be detecting the effect. But the step from “the mean shifts” to “individual raters are caught” is exactly the step that needs unpacking — and unpacking it changes the picture.
crumbs: 5  What did we actually measure? Sensitivity vs. a shift in means

title: 5  What did we actually measure? Sensitivity vs. a shift in means
text:
5.2 Why this threatens the conclusion
The gap is clearest by analogy. Consider a school nurse with a thermometer, asked to find which children have a fever. Detection asks two questions: of the children who truly have a fever, what fraction does the thermometer flag (the true-positive rate, TPR)? And of the healthy children, what fraction does it flag by mistake (the false-positive rate, FPR)? The ROC curve traces the trade-off between those two across every possible cut score, and the area under it (AUC) summarizes the whole thing in one number — 1.0 is perfect, 0.5 is a coin flip. (Geometrically: plot TPR against FPR as the cut slides from strict to lax; a perfect detector hugs the top-left corner, AUC = 1, while a coin flip lies along the diagonal, AUC = 0.5.)
Partial η² answers a different question. It quantifies how much of the variance in thermometer readings is explained by whether the child is sick. A thermometer can show a large group-mean gap — sick kids average 39°C, healthy kids 37°C — while still flagging one in six healthy children, because normal temperatures vary and the cut falls inside the normal range. The big η² says the thermometer is sensitive to sickness as a population-level signal; it does not say the thermometer is accurate at the individual-level decision.
This is not hypothetical. The measurement model was reconstructed in R from the simulation parameters described in the dissertation — not a re-run of TAM, but a ground-up implementation. In a severity scenario at the field-standard +0.5-logit cut, the reconstruction recovers η² ≈ 0.80 and AUC ≈ 0.997 — both excellent. But the false-positive rate at that cut is about 16%, because normal-rater severities are drawn from N(0, 0.5) and +0.5 falls exactly one standard deviation above the mean, so by construction about one in six normal raters clears it. That 16% is never visible in the dissertation, because analyzing effect raters only makes the false-positive side of the problem structurally absent from the data. Figure 5.1 makes this concrete.


Show the R code (severity detection + η²→AUC map)
suppressPackageStartupMessages({ library(ggplot2); library(patchwork); library(scales) })
source("R/common.R")
set.seed(303)

# model-information weight W = average score variance; SE of a rater location
d_vals <- rnorm(5, 0, 0.5); thL <- rnorm(40000)
W  <- mean(vapply(d_vals, function(d) mean(score_variance(pcmfrm_probs(thL, 0, d, alpha = 1))), numeric(1)))
se_lambda <- function(N, I = 5) 1 / sqrt(N * I * W)

# ---- Panel A: severity detection at N=100, cut = +0.5 -----------------------
SE <- se_lambda(100); CUT <- 0.5; NR <- 8000
lam_norm <- rnorm(NR, 0, 0.5) + rnorm(NR, 0, SE)
lam_sev  <- rnorm(NR, 2, 0.5) + rnorm(NR, 0, SE)
eta2_two <- function(a, b) { g <- c(a, b); gm <- mean(g)
  (length(a)*(mean(a)-gm)^2 + length(b)*(mean(b)-gm)^2) / sum((g-gm)^2) }
eta2_A <- eta2_two(lam_norm, lam_sev)
TPR_A <- mean(lam_sev > CUT); FPR_A <- mean(lam_norm > CUT); AUC_A <- mean(outer(lam_sev, lam_norm, ">"))

xs <- seq(-1.5, 5.5, length.out = 600); sdmix <- sqrt(0.5^2 + SE^2)
dens <- rbind(data.frame(x = xs, y = dnorm(xs, 0, sdmix), g = "Normal raters (~N(0, 0.5))"),
              data.frame(x = xs, y = dnorm(xs, 2, sdmix), g = "Severe raters (~N(2, 0.5))"))
dens$g <- factor(dens$g, levels = c("Normal raters (~N(0, 0.5))", "Severe raters (~N(2, 0.5))"))
fp <- subset(dens, g == "Normal raters (~N(0, 0.5))" & x >= CUT)
box <- sprintf("η² = %.2f   (huge effect)\nAUC = %.3f\nTPR = %.3f\nFPR = %.3f  ← not reported here\n          (effect raters only)",
               eta2_A, AUC_A, TPR_A, FPR_A)
pA <- ggplot(dens, aes(x, y, colour = g)) +
  geom_area(data = fp, aes(x, y), fill = pal_companion[["none"]], alpha = .4, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = CUT, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey30", linewidth = .7) +
  annotate("text", x = CUT + .08, y = max(dens$y) * .85, label = "+0.5-logit\ncut", hjust = 0,
           size = 3, colour = "grey30", lineheight = .85) +
  annotate("text", x = 1.55, y = .10, label = "false\npositives", size = 3, colour = pal_companion[["none"]],
           fontface = "bold", lineheight = .9) +
  annotate("label", x = 5.4, y = max(dens$y) * .62, label = box, hjust = 1, vjust = 1,
           size = 2.85, lineheight = 1.1, fill = "white", colour = "grey20") +
  scale_colour_manual(name = NULL, values = c("Normal raters (~N(0, 0.5))" = pal_companion[["none"]],
                                              "Severe raters (~N(2, 0.5))" = pal_companion[["severe"]])) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-1, 5, 1)) +
  labs(title = "(A)  A huge η² hides a 16% false-positive rate",
       subtitle = "estimated rater location (logits); N = 100 persons per rater",
       x = "estimated rater severity  λ̂  (logits)", y = "density") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(legend.position = "bottom",
                              plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9))

# ---- Panel B: η² -> detection map -------------------------------------------
set.seed(303)
dfB <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq(0.05, 3, length.out = 40), function(mu) {
  a <- rnorm(6000, 0, .5); b <- rnorm(6000, mu, .5)
  data.frame(eta2 = eta2_two(a, b), auc = mean(outer(b, a, ">")), tpr = mean(b > CUT)) }))
i01 <- which.min(abs(dfB$eta2 - 0.01)); auc01 <- dfB$auc[i01]
eta2_seq <- seq(1e-4, .92, length.out = 400); d_seq <- 2 * sqrt(eta2_seq / (1 - eta2_seq))
ana <- subset(data.frame(eta2 = eta2_seq, auc = pnorm(d_seq / sqrt(2))), auc >= .45)
pB <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = ana, aes(eta2, auc), colour = pal_companion[["misfit"]], linewidth = .6, linetype = "dotted") +
  geom_line(data = dfB, aes(eta2, auc, colour = "ROC-AUC"), linewidth = .9) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = .5, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey55", linewidth = .65) +
  annotate("text", x = .0011, y = .505, label = "AUC = 0.5  (chance)", hjust = 0, vjust = 0, size = 2.9, colour = "grey45") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = .01, linetype = "dashed", colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], linewidth = .75) +
  annotate("text", x = .013, y = .63, hjust = 0, vjust = .5, size = 2.85, lineheight = .9,
           colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], fontface = "italic",
           label = sprintf("η² = .01\n\"practical\nsignificance\"\n→ AUC ≈ %.2f\n(near chance)", auc01)) +
  geom_point(data = dfB[i01, ], aes(eta2, auc), colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], size = 3) +
  scale_x_log10(breaks = c(.001, .01, .05, .1, .3, .6, .9), labels = c(".001", ".01", ".05", ".10", ".30", ".60", ".90")) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(.45, 1.01), breaks = seq(.5, 1, .1), oob = scales::squish) +
  scale_colour_manual(name = NULL, values = c("ROC-AUC" = pal_companion[["severe"]])) +
  labs(title = "(B)  η² ≥ .01 ≠ ability to detect a rater",
       subtitle = "two groups N(0, 0.5) vs N(μ, 0.5); dotted = analytic AUC = Φ(d/√2)",
       x = "partial η²  (log scale)", y = "detection metric") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(legend.position = "bottom",
                              plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9),
                              panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
pA | pB








Figure 5.1: η² is not detection. (A) Even with a huge η² (0.80) and an excellent AUC (0.997), the field-standard +0.5-logit cut flags ≈ 16% of normal raters as severe (shaded tail) — a false-positive rate the effect-raters-only analysis never reports. (B) Across conditions, the study’s η² ≥ .01 ‘practical significance’ bar lands at AUC ≈ 0.56 — barely above the 0.5 chance line. A large mean shift and good individual detection are different things.





The η² ≥ .01 threshold compounds the problem. A partial η² of .01 corresponds to Cohen’s \(d \approx 0.20\), which corresponds to AUC ≈ 0.557 — barely distinguishable from chance (Panel B). The bar says “practically significant,” but it is set so low that an index can clear it while doing almost no useful individual-level discrimination. The figure does not indict the findings; it shows that η² and AUC track different things, and that a single bar should not be asked to certify both.
crumbs: 5  What did we actually measure? Sensitivity vs. a shift in means

title: 5  What did we actually measure? Sensitivity vs. a shift in means
text:
5.3 What would fix it






TipFix-it



The revision is more additive than corrective — the mean-shift results stay; they just need company.

Report detection directly — the full confusion-matrix readout. For each indicator and condition, give, at each stated cut, the true-positive rate (sensitivity — fraction of effect raters correctly flagged), the false-positive rate (fraction of normal raters wrongly flagged) and its complement specificity (1 − FPR), plus the predictive values that tell a reader what a flag is worth — PPV (of the raters flagged, the fraction that truly have an effect) and NPV (of those cleared, the fraction truly normal). Add the threshold-free summary, the ROC curve with its AUC, and a calibration check: does the realized flag rate among normal raters match the nominal rate the cut is meant to deliver (a +0.5-logit cut on \(N(0, 0.5)\) should flag ≈ 16%, not whatever an off-target cut produces)? And because TPR, FPR, and AUC are themselves averages over the 100 replications, report a Monte-Carlo standard error for each, so a reader can tell a genuine difference between indicators from simulation noise.
Pre-specify a direction-aware flag rule, then report detection at it. Severity has a sign: a one-sided +0.5-logit cut catches only over-severity and is structurally blind to leniency (the negative tail), so a too-lenient rater is missed no matter how large the effect. Decide the rule before looking at the results — pre-register a two-sided severity/leniency flag (\(|\hat{\lambda}| > c\)), or a pair of explicit one-sided cuts (\(+c\) for severity, \(-c\) for leniency, reported separately) — and report TPR/FPR/AUC at that pre-registered rule rather than at a cut chosen after seeing which side the effect fell on.
Include both normal and effect raters. The false-positive rate is undefined if normal raters are excluded — so the analysis must contain them.
Add parameter recovery already latent in the design: bias, RMSE, and coverage of the rater-effect estimates — how well the index estimates, alongside whether it flags.
Justify or replace the η² ≥ .01 bar. If kept, say plainly that it marks a non-trivial mean shift, not acceptable detection accuracy; better, report AUC next to η².
Reserve “sensitivity” for the detection quantity (TPR / ROC / AUC), and describe the η² results as “how much the mean index shifts across conditions” — a real, reportable finding, but a different claim.
crumbs: 5  What did we actually measure? Sensitivity vs. a shift in means

title: 5  What did we actually measure? Sensitivity vs. a shift in means
text:
5.4 What a reviewer will ask






WarningWhat a reviewer will ask



“Your definition of sensitivity is detection — correctly identifying problem raters. Why report the partial η² of the mean index on effect raters only, rather than the true-positive and false-positive rates and the ROC-AUC at your cut scores? And what justifies an η² ≥ .01 ‘practical significance’ bar that corresponds to near-chance detection?”


A prepared answer is available: the η² analysis was a condition-sensitivity check — showing the index responds to an effect at all — but individual-level detection requires TPR, FPR, and AUC, and the revised manuscript can report them directly. That response frames the revision as a strengthening, not a correction. This chapter unlocks the others: once “sensitivity” means detection and is measured as detection, the earlier concerns about ψ, the misfit manipulation, and the MCMC all become easier to state precisely and to answer.
crumbs: 5  What did we actually measure? Sensitivity vs. a shift in means

title: 6  Local dependence: marginal correlation vs. residual correlation
text:
6.1 What the dissertation does
Chapter 4 takes seriously a threat (rater dependence) that most applied MFRM studies ignore, and builds a simulation to probe it. The revision concerns sharpening the interpretation so the study says precisely what it shows, and no more. The relevant pages are 76–90.
Local independence — in Yen’s (1993) formulation, as quoted on page 76 — is a property of residual correlations: a pair of scores is locally independent when they are “independent after controlling for the latent variable.” In a many-facet Rasch context that means two raters’ scores on the same examinees should be uncorrelated once examinee ability, rater severity, and item difficulty have been accounted for. The residual is whatever shared signal remains after the model has explained what it can.
Chapter 4 asks whether MFRM indices are sensitive to violations of that property. The design on page 84 selects rater pairs so that about 20% of raters belong to a correlated pair, then “replaced one rater’s scores with values that had a specific correlation with their partner’s scores” at three target levels — 0.2, 0.6, and 0.9 — describing this as reflecting “Yen’s (1993) definition of local independence as low correlations among items after controlling for examinee ability” (pp. 84–85). The result, on page 90, is that “the practical impact of rater dependence was minimal across conditions, suggesting that the MFRM is robust to this type of potential local independence violation.”
The logic is clean and the implementation careful. The one problem is that the manipulation operates on a marginal (raw, unconditional) correlation between the two raters’ scores — not on the residual correlation the conclusion invokes. And because that marginal baseline depends on the unit (total score vs. single item), even the sign of the dependence some conditions impose turns out to be ambiguous.
crumbs: 6  Local dependence: marginal correlation vs. residual correlation

title: 6  Local dependence: marginal correlation vs. residual correlation
text:
6.2 Why this threatens the conclusion
Start from first principles. Imagine two raters, A and B, scoring the same pool of examinees independently — no consultation, no shared bias. Under perfect local independence their residual correlation is zero by definition. Yet both are doing the same job: each tracks examinee ability. When ability is high, both tend to award higher scores; when it is low, both score lower. That shared pull from the latent variable creates a positive marginal correlation between A and B even when they are perfectly locally independent.
Think of two thermometers hanging in the same room. Neither influences the other, but both respond to the room temperature, so their readings move together. The correlation is not evidence of dependence between the thermometers — it is evidence of the shared environment they both measure. Rater A and rater B are two thermometers; examinee ability is the room.
The magnitude was verified by simulating two locally-independent partial-credit MFRM raters scoring the same examinees in R — not a re-run of TAM, but a ground-up construction. Under perfect local independence the marginal correlation of their total scores was about 0.79 — and of their ratings on a single shared item, about 0.43 — while a residual correlation after partialling out ability was about 0.03, indistinguishable from zero. That ≈ 0.03 is a simplified illustration of the residual idea — the partial correlation of the two raters’ total scores given ability — rather than Yen’s Q3 itself: Yen’s (1993) Q3 proper correlates the item-level residuals from the fitted model (observed minus model-expected for each person × item) across item or rater pairs. The illustration still makes the point — under local independence the residual sits near zero — but a real check would report Q3. Both marginal numbers matter; the targets land differently against each. Figure 6.1 makes the geometry visible.


Show the R code (two locally-independent raters; marginal vs residual)
suppressPackageStartupMessages({ library(ggplot2); library(patchwork) })
source("R/common.R")
set.seed(7)

I <- 5L; N_EX <- 500L; N_REP <- 400L; TARGETS <- c(0.2, 0.6, 0.9)
delta <- rnorm(I, 0, 0.5)

partial_corr <- function(x, y, z) {                  # corr(x, y | z), z quadratic
  Z <- cbind(1, z, z^2); cor(lm.fit(Z, x)$residuals, lm.fit(Z, y)$residuals)
}
two_rater_totals <- function(n) {                    # two LI raters, same examinees
  th <- rnorm(n); l1 <- rnorm(1, 0, .5); l2 <- rnorm(1, 0, .5)
  R1 <- R2 <- matrix(0, n, I)                        # per-item ratings, both raters
  for (i in seq_len(I)) { R1[, i] <- simulate_rating(pcmfrm_probs(th, l1, delta[i]))
                          R2[, i] <- simulate_rating(pcmfrm_probs(th, l2, delta[i])) }
  list(t1 = rowSums(R1), t2 = rowSums(R2), theta = th, R1 = R1, R2 = R2)
}
marg <- res <- item_base <- numeric(N_REP)
for (r in seq_len(N_REP)) { o <- two_rater_totals(N_EX)
  marg[r] <- cor(o$t1, o$t2); res[r] <- partial_corr(o$t1, o$t2, o$theta)
  item_base[r] <- mean(sapply(seq_len(I), function(i) cor(o$R1[, i], o$R2[, i]))) }
baseline <- mean(marg); sdm <- sd(marg); mres <- mean(res); sdr <- sd(res)
baseline_item <- mean(item_base)               # item/rating-level LI baseline ≈ 0.43

# Panel A: sampling distribution of the marginal correlation under LI
pA <- ggplot(data.frame(r = marg), aes(r)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0.08, xmax = baseline_item, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = pal_companion[["severe"]], alpha = .07) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = baseline_item, xmax = baseline, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = pal_companion[["warn"]], alpha = .07) +
  annotate("text", x = (0.08 + baseline_item)/2, y = 21, label = "below BOTH\n→ neg. residual",
           vjust = 1, size = 2.6, colour = pal_companion[["severe"]], fontface = "italic", lineheight = .9) +
  annotate("text", x = (baseline_item + baseline)/2, y = 21, label = "between baselines\n→ scale-dependent",
           vjust = 1, size = 2.6, colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], fontface = "italic", lineheight = .9) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), binwidth = .01, fill = pal_companion[["none"]],
                 colour = "white", alpha = .85) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = baseline_item, colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = baseline, colour = pal_companion[["accent"]], linewidth = 1.1) +
  annotate("text", x = baseline_item - .012, y = 14, hjust = 1, size = 2.7, fontface = "bold",
           colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], label = sprintf("item baseline\nr ≈ %.2f", baseline_item)) +
  annotate("text", x = baseline + .012, y = 16, hjust = 0, size = 2.7, fontface = "bold",
           colour = pal_companion[["accent"]], label = sprintf("total baseline\nr ≈ %.2f", baseline)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = TARGETS, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = c(pal_companion[["severe"]], pal_companion[["warn"]], pal_companion[["misfit"]]), linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("label", x = .2, y = 11, size = 2.6, fill = "white", colour = pal_companion[["severe"]],
           label = "r = 0.2\nbelow both\n→ neg. residual") +
  annotate("label", x = .58, y = 6, size = 2.6, fill = "white", colour = pal_companion[["warn"]],
           label = "r = 0.6\nbetween → violation\nat item level") +
  annotate("label", x = .9, y = 11, size = 2.6, fill = "white", colour = pal_companion[["misfit"]],
           label = "r = 0.9\nabove both\n→ genuine violation") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(.2, 1, .2), limits = c(.06, .98)) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, .05))) +
  labs(title = "(A)  The LI baseline depends on the unit",
       subtitle = "marginal corr(rater 1, rater 2) under perfect local independence (500 examinees, 400 reps)",
       x = "marginal correlation between raters", y = "density") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 8.5))

# Panel B: marginal vs residual correlation
barB <- data.frame(type = factor(c("marginal", "residual"), levels = c("marginal", "residual")),
                   v = c(baseline, mres), lo = c(baseline - 2*sdm, mres - 2*sdr), hi = c(baseline + 2*sdm, mres + 2*sdr))
pB <- ggplot(barB, aes(type, v, fill = type)) +
  geom_col(width = .5, alpha = .85) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi), width = .12, colour = pal_companion[["none"]]) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", v)), vjust = -0.6, size = 4.3, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("label", x = 1.5, y = .45, size = 2.85, fill = "white", colour = pal_companion[["misfit"]],
           label = "Yen (1993) dependence\n= the residual bar,\nnot the marginal one") +
  annotate("segment", x = 1.78, xend = 1.93, y = .42, yend = mres + .07,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(.15, "cm"), type = "closed"), colour = pal_companion[["misfit"]], linewidth = .8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey60", linewidth = .5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(marginal = unname(pal_companion[["none"]]), residual = unname(pal_companion[["misfit"]])), guide = "none") +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c(marginal = "marginal\ncor(T1, T2)", residual = "residual\n(given ability θ)")) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-.05, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, .2)) +
  labs(title = "(B)  Marginal r ≈ 0.79;  residual r ≈ 0.03",
       subtitle = "shared θ drives the marginal correlation", x = NULL, y = "correlation") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 8.5))

(pA | pB) + plot_layout(widths = c(1.5, 1))








Figure 6.1: Marginal correlation is not local dependence — and the baseline depends on the unit. (A) Under perfect local independence, two raters scoring the same examinees are already correlated at ≈ 0.79 on their 5-item total scores but only ≈ 0.43 on the same single item (the unit Yen’s ‘low correlations among items’ names). Read against both baselines, the targets split three ways: 0.2 falls below both (reaching it needs negative residual dependence — the opposite of a violation); 0.9 sits above both (a genuine positive violation); and 0.6 sits between them — above the item baseline but below the total — so its verdict depends on scale, a genuine positive violation under the item/rating reading the ‘among items’ wording implies. (B) Under local independence the total-score marginal correlation is ≈ 0.79 but the residual correlation given ability is ≈ 0.03 — and Yen’s definition is about the residual (right) bar, not the marginal (left) one.





The targets must be read against that baseline — but first which baseline must be decided, because it depends on the unit correlated. Two raters’ 5-item total scores are marginally correlated at ≈ 0.79 under perfect local independence; their ratings on the same single item are correlated at only ≈ 0.43, because the shared-ability signal accumulates across five items while a lone ordinal rating carries far less of it. This distinction is decisive, because the dissertation fixes the unit at the item level: page 84 describes “replac[ing] one rater’s scores,” and pages 84–85 ground the design in “Yen’s (1993) definition of local independence as low correlations among items.” Among items — so the baseline that matters is the per-item one (≈ 0.43), not the total-score one (≈ 0.79).
Read against both baselines, the three targets split three ways rather than two (Panel A):

0.2 sits below both baselines. Reaching it requires injecting negative residual correlation — suppressing the natural ability-driven co-movement, as if the raters were anti-coordinated after controlling for ability. On either unit, this is the opposite of a positive violation.
0.9 sits above both baselines — a genuine positive violation on either reading.
0.6 sits between the two: below the total-score baseline of 0.79, but above the item-level baseline of 0.43. Its verdict therefore depends on the scale the manipulation operated on. Read at the total-score level it would still imply mild negative residual dependence; but read at the item/rating level — the reading the dissertation’s own “among items” wording implies — 0.6 is a genuine positive violation, and a near-null MFRM response would mean the model absorbed a real violation rather than that none was there.

That cuts partly in the dissertation’s favour. On the more defensible (item-level) reading, the 0.6 and 0.9 conditions did impose genuine positive dependence, so a null result is not merely an artefact of removing co-movement. What the design still cannot settle is which scale was in force — the manipulation’s unit was never stated, and the residual correlation that actually entered the MFRM was never measured. So a null result remains consistent with several stories (the MFRM is genuinely robust; the 0.2 condition imposed negative dependence and could never have flagged; or the raw-score replacement created an item-level structure the model quietly absorbed), and the data as they stand cannot tell them apart.
There is a quieter issue too: overwriting one rater’s scores with a constructed vector changes not just the pair correlation but that rater’s score distribution and severity estimate — so the residual correlation that actually entered the MFRM (the quantity Yen’s definition is about) was never directly measured.






NoteThe strongest counterargument



In the dissertation’s defense, the manipulation follows Yen’s (1993) “low correlations among items” framing directly — it is grounded in his definition and operates at the item level, the unit his wording names. It does genuinely move the rater-pair correlation; the conditions are not inert. And a near-null MFRM response is not nothing — it is positive, informative evidence about the model’s robustness to the induced dependence.
And yet — granted, and this cuts partly in the dissertation’s favour: under the item-level reading, the 0.6 and 0.9 conditions sit above the ≈ 0.43 baseline (Panel A) and so are genuine positive violations the MFRM apparently absorbed, which is a point in its favour, not against it. What the design cannot yet settle is the cleanness of the reading, for two stated reasons — the manipulation’s scale was never declared, and the realized residual correlation that actually entered the MFRM was never measured. Until both are reported (ideally as Yen’s Q3 per condition), a flat result stays equally consistent with “the MFRM is robust” and with “the 0.2 condition imposed negative dependence that could never flag.” Reporting the realized residual per condition is the one addition that turns an ambiguous null into a measured dose — and on the item-level reading, it likely settles the matter in the dissertation’s favour.
crumbs: 6  Local dependence: marginal correlation vs. residual correlation

title: 6  Local dependence: marginal correlation vs. residual correlation
text:
6.3 What would fix it






TipFix-it




Report realized correlations at both scales. Per condition, give (a) the realized marginal correlation — at both the total-score and the item/rating level — and (b) the realized residual correlation, also at both levels: Yen’s Q3 — the correlation of item-level residuals from the fitted MFRM (observed minus model-expected per person × item) — which is the proper diagnostic, shown beside the total-score partial correlation given ability (the ≈ 0.03 illustration above) for comparison. Q3 is the quantity that settles each condition; the total-score partial is reported only so a reader can see the two readings agree. This one addition lets a reviewer see where each condition truly sits.
Re-manipulate on the residual scale. Induce dependence directly in residual space — a shared rater-pair random effect added to the log-odds (cleanest), a residual copula, a testlet-style MFRM pair effect, or a training-group random effect — sweeping a known residual correlation across, say, −0.3 to +0.5 around the LI baseline of 0.
Separate two phenomena. Repeated-ratings dependence (the same script scored twice) and contextual dependence (a shared training cohort) have different residual structures; distinguishing them sharpens the contribution.
Compare with dependence-aware models. Ask not only whether MFRM flags the dependence but whether a rater-bundle model, random-effect facet model, testlet MFRM, or HRM recovers parameters better when genuine positive residual dependence is present.
Re-scope the conclusion. State it as “MFRM indices changed little under this specific score-replacement manipulation” — noting that on the item-level reading the 0.6 and 0.9 conditions plausibly did impose positive dependence the model absorbed — not “robust to local-independence violations” in general.
crumbs: 6  Local dependence: marginal correlation vs. residual correlation

title: 6  Local dependence: marginal correlation vs. residual correlation
text:
6.4 What a reviewer will ask






WarningWhat a reviewer will ask



“Could you report the realized residual correlation given ability in each condition, and state the unit your target correlations were defined on? Two raters scoring the same examinees are already marginally correlated under perfect local independence — at ≈ 0.79 on their total scores, but only ≈ 0.43 on a single shared item. Since you cite Yen’s ‘low correlations among items,’ the item-level baseline of ≈ 0.43 looks like the relevant one — and on that reading, 0.2 sits below it (negative residual dependence, not a violation) while 0.6 and 0.9 sit above it and are genuine positive violations. Could you confirm which scale the manipulation operated on, and report the realized residual correlation per condition, so a null result can be read as ‘the MFRM absorbed real positive dependence’ rather than left ambiguous?”


Whether the MFRM holds up when raters are not independent is a question the field needs answered, and this study builds the machinery to answer it. The fix is small relative to the idea — move the manipulation onto the residual scale, report the residual correlation actually induced, and let the conclusion follow. Done that way, this becomes a stronger paper than a clean null would have been: a study that pins down precisely how much residual dependence the MFRM can absorb, with the dose measured on the scale Yen actually defined.
crumbs: 6  Local dependence: marginal correlation vs. residual correlation

title: 7  Additional concerns
text:
7.1 The condition count doesn’t add up (a reproducibility flag)
The issues gathered here are smaller in scope than those in the earlier chapters — think of it as housekeeping. Four independent problems, each with a clear fix, none of which threatens the dissertation’s central argument. Sorting them out before submission makes the manuscript sharper and forestalls the kind of reviewer queries that slow a review without adding anything substantive.
What happens. Table 18 (p. 84) defines the Study 3 grid along four factors: sample size (50, 100, 500 — three levels), data-collection design (complete, incomplete, anchor — three), effect type (none, severity, misfit — three), and rater correlation (0.2, 0.6, 0.9 — three). That is 3 × 3 × 3 × 3 = 81 conditions. Yet the text says “we created 108 combinations of simulation conditions” (p. 85), and the Discussion quietly introduces a fourth sample size — “n = 50, 100, 500, and 900” (p. 89) — that appears nowhere in the methods or in Table 18. The arithmetic resolves immediately: 108 = 4 × 3 × 3 × 3, so the only route to 108 is a fourth sample-size level, which is exactly the stray n = 900.
Show the arithmetic
cat("Table 18 grid:  3 × 3 × 3 × 3 =", 3^4, "conditions\n")


#> Table 18 grid:  3 × 3 × 3 × 3 = 81 conditions


Show the arithmetic
cat("Stated in text:                 108 conditions\n")


#> Stated in text:                 108 conditions


Show the arithmetic
cat("108 = 4 × 3 × 3 × 3 =", 4 * 3^3, " → needs a 4th sample size (the stray n = 900)\n\n")


#> 108 = 4 × 3 × 3 × 3 = 108  → needs a 4th sample size (the stray n = 900)


Show the arithmetic
# A "count" framing of the false-flag problem at the TWO-SIDED ±0.5-logit cut: how many
# NORMAL raters (true severity ~ N(0, 0.5)) are flagged by chance as too severe OR too
# lenient (|estimated λ| > 0.5, either direction)? This two-sided rate (~0.318) is ~2×
# Chapter 5's ONE-sided +0.5 false-positive rate (~0.159); the count below is R × 0.318.
fp <- data.frame(R = c(5, 10, 50))                      # raters in the dataset
fp$N    <- fp$R * 10                                    # examinees (raters = N/10)
fp$se   <- 1 / sqrt(fp$N * 5 * 0.77)                    # SE of a location (0.77 ≈ avg score variance W, cf. Ch.5/Ch.2)
fp$rate <- 2 * (1 - pnorm(0.5 / sqrt(0.5^2 + fp$se^2))) # P(|estimated λ| > 0.5)
fp$falsely_flagged <- round(fp$R * fp$rate, 1)
fp[, c("R", "rate", "falsely_flagged")]


#>    R      rate falsely_flagged
#> 1  5 0.3222868             1.6
#> 2 10 0.3198115             3.2
#> 3 50 0.3178128            15.9
At R = 50 raters, the table’s analytic expected count is roughly 16 normal raters tripping the ±0.5-logit flag by chance alone — where “flagged” means estimated too severe or too lenient, i.e. |λ| > 0.5 in either direction (a two-sided count). This is the legible “how-many-raters” companion to Chapter 5’s false-positive rate — but the two are not the same number wearing different clothes. Chapter 5’s ≈ 16% is a one-sided rate (the +0.5 tail of N(0, 0.5)), which on its own is ≈ 8 of 50 raters; counting flags in either direction roughly doubles the per-rater rate to ≈ 0.32, giving the ≈ 16 here. The matching “16”s are therefore a coincidence — Chapter 5’s 16 percent and this chapter’s 16 raters are different quantities that happen to land on the same digits.
Why it matters. Study 3 is entirely a simulation, so the condition grid is the empirical basis. Any inconsistency among what Table 18 lists, what the text claims, and what the ANOVAs actually processed means the results cannot be fully reproduced from the manuscript alone — readers cannot verify which cells fed the analyses, and the appendix tables become hard to interpret without knowing whether n = 900 was run.
Fix. Correct the count (to 81 or 108, whichever was actually run); add n = 900 to Table 18 if those cells were generated, or remove the Discussion reference if they were not; and release the condition log or simulation script so the grid is machine-readable and independently verifiable.
crumbs: Part II — Additional concerns & the path forward

title: 7  Additional concerns
section: 7.2 Two names for the Study 3 generating model
text:
7.2 Two names for the Study 3 generating model
What happens. The Study 3 design states that ratings were generated from “a modified version of the MFRM that allows us to generate certain types of rater effects” (p. 84), written out as Equation (8) — a many-facet model with rater severity \(\lambda_j\), rater discrimination \(\alpha_j\), item difficulty \(\delta_i\), and thresholds \(\tau_{ik}\). Yet a few lines down on the same page, the local-independence factor is described as starting from ratings simulated under a different model: “After simulating baseline ratings using the GPCM, we randomly selected rater pairs…” (p. 84). The two phrases name two different data-generating models. The GPCM (Muraki, 1992) is an item-level polytomous model — item discrimination \(a_i\), no rater facets — whereas the adapted MFRM of Equation (8) is a many-facet model built around rater severity and rater discrimination. In the measurement literature these are not interchangeable labels for one model.
Why it matters. Study 3 is a simulation, so the generating model is the data. As written, a reader cannot tell whether the local-independence cells were built on the Equation (8) many-facet model the rest of the chapter implies, or on a genuinely different item-level GPCM — with the rater-pair dependence then injected on top of whichever baseline was used. That ambiguity blocks exact reproduction of the very condition this study is named for. It is almost certainly a wording slip rather than two real pipelines — the dissertation uses the GPCM legitimately elsewhere as the HRM’s first stage — but only a one-word correction can settle it for the reader.
Fix. State the generating model once, consistently, and use that name throughout Study 3. If (as is almost certain) Equation (8) is the intended baseline, replace “GPCM” on p. 84 with the adapted-MFRM / Equation (8) reference; if a separate GPCM baseline really was used for the local-independence factor, give its equation and say explicitly why it differs from Equation (8). Either way, one named model end-to-end removes the ambiguity at no cost to the findings.
crumbs: Part II — Additional concerns & the path forward

title: 7  Additional concerns
text:
7.3 Strengthening the ANOVA analysis
What happens. Each indicator is analyzed with a separate univariate ANOVA, and practical significance is declared when partial η² ≥ .01, on data with 100 replications per condition and multiple raters nested within each replication.
Why it matters. Three problems compound. First, raters within a replication share a common data-generating context and are not independent; the ANOVA treats them as if they were, understating standard errors. Second, the indicators are correlated outcomes analyzed in parallel with no multivariate test or multiplicity adjustment, which inflates the family-wise error rate invisibly. Third — and most consequential — with 100 replications × many raters, p-values are almost always significant, so the entire interpretation rests on the η² ≥ .01 bar, which (as Chapter 5 showed) corresponds to near-chance detection. The ANOVAs may be certifying “practically significant” factors whose practical contribution is negligible.
Fix. Analyze replicate-level summaries (collapsing within each replication first), or use a mixed-effects model with replication as a random factor — standard in simulation methodology and familiar to reviewers. Pre-specify one or two primary outcomes (or apply a multiplicity correction across the battery of ANOVAs), and report recovery/detection metrics alongside — or instead of — the omnibus F and η², so effect sizes can be read on a scale with direct practical meaning.
crumbs: Part II — Additional concerns & the path forward

title: 7  Additional concerns
section: 7.4 Coding reliability of the systematic review
text:
7.4 Coding reliability of the systematic review
What happens. The systematic review that opens the dissertation was coded by a single person: “I coded all articles based on the following criteria…” (p. 13), with disagreements “resolved through discussion with my Dissertation Chair until a consensus was reached” (pp. 13–14). No inter-coder reliability statistic — no Cohen’s κ, no percent agreement — is reported.
Why it matters. The systematic review’s contribution is its frequency tables characterizing the rater-effects literature, so their credibility rests on the coding. Let me be explicit that this is strictly additive, not fatal: the review is a genuine, keepable contribution — real work and real scholarship. But journal reviewers in measurement and applied linguistics routinely expect either a second independent coder with a reported reliability statistic on a sample of articles, or a documented coding audit; the current description gives no basis for assessing how consistently the criteria were applied.
Fix. Double-code a random ~20% sample of the articles independently, compute κ and percent agreement for each major category, and report them in the methods. If that is not feasible before submission, a transparent coding audit — a supplementary codebook documenting decision rules and borderline cases — will satisfy most reviewers.






TipFix-it




Condition count. Reconcile the stated 108 with Table 18’s 81-cell grid; add n = 900 to Table 18 if those cells were run (or remove the reference); release the condition log.
Study 3 generating model. State one model end-to-end: p. 84 names both the Equation (8) adapted MFRM and the GPCM as the baseline that produced the ratings. If Equation (8) is intended (almost certainly), drop the stray “GPCM”; otherwise give the GPCM baseline its own equation and rationale.
ANOVA hygiene. Analyze replicate-level summaries (or mixed-effects models) to respect within-replication clustering; pre-specify primary outcomes or correct for multiplicity; report recovery/detection rates alongside F and η².
Coding reliability. Double-code ~20% of the systematic-review articles and report Cohen’s κ / percent agreement, or supply a supplementary codebook documenting the decision rules.

And one reporting standard ties the simulation work together: attach a Monte-Carlo standard error to every number both studies average over the 100 replications — Study 2’s and Study 3’s condition means, detection/recovery rates, and AUC alike (the across-replication SD over \(\sqrt{100}\) for a mean, the binomial MC-SE for a rate), reported beside the quantity in the tables — so a reader can separate a real between-condition difference from simulation noise rather than reading 100-run averages as exact. It is the replication-level companion to the posterior MCSE Chapter 4 asks for at the estimation level, and generalizes the MC-SE Chapter 5 attaches to its detection metrics.


All four are fixable in an afternoon — none changes the dissertation’s central findings; each makes the manuscript meaningfully more defensible to a skeptical reviewer.

title: 8  A minimal-revision roadmap (and the questions to expect)
text:
8.1 The minimum that lets the central conclusions stand
The dissertation is done, and the committee approved it. What follows is not a verdict on that work — it is a runway for what comes next. The central arguments about MFRM and HRM sensitivity and robustness are worth publishing; the only question is how to present them so a peer reviewer — who has not met the author and will read quickly — cannot find a foothold for doubt. This chapter pulls the seven critique chapters into one sequenced plan, then gathers the questions a reviewer is most likely to ask.
The headline claims do not require new data. They require tighter grounding in what the reported numbers actually measure. None of what follows requires collecting a single new rating or rebuilding a study from scratch — every fix is a targeted addition or a reframing: better diagnostics where the estimation went unchecked, cleaner operationalizations where the manipulations drifted from their definitions, and modest housekeeping where the reporting left questions open. The architecture of both simulation studies (the dissertation’s Chapters 3 and 4) is sound; tighten the scaffolding and the same intellectual argument becomes airtight.
crumbs: Part II — Additional concerns & the path forward
8  A minimal-revision roadmap (and the questions to expect)

title: 8  A minimal-revision roadmap (and the questions to expect)
text:
8.2 A sequenced plan
(“Ch.” below refers to the chapters of this companion, not the dissertation; the order follows logical dependency rather than chapter number.)
Work in this order, because each tier clears the ground for the next. Tier 1 comes first because if “sensitivity” is measuring the wrong thing (a) or the HRM estimates are unstable (b), none of the other numbers can be interpreted at all. Tier 2 then fixes what the manipulations actually estimate, and Tier 3 is genuine housekeeping — real, but not blocking.

8.2.1 Tier 1 — Reframe and re-estimate (highest leverage)

(a) Re-operationalize “sensitivity” as detection. For every condition, report TPR, FPR, and ROC-AUC at the cut score (and parameter recovery), with both normal and effect raters in the analysis so the false-positive rate is visible; retire or explicitly justify the η² ≥ .01 bar. This single move reframes both studies and answers the most fundamental objection (Ch. 5). Effort: medium — one analysis pass, no new simulation.
(b) Re-estimate the HRM with adequate MCMC. Run ≥ 4 independent chains with a longer warm-up; report R-hat, bulk/tail ESS, and MCSE for φ and ψ in every condition; do a prior-sensitivity check with a log-scale prior for ψ; and flag the misfit ψ as non-estimable rather than reporting a sampler-generated value (Ch. 1 + Ch. 4). Effort: higher — re-running estimation is the bottleneck; the model itself does not change.
(c) Diagnose the severe-location collapse. Show parameter recovery (bias, RMSE, coverage) for the severe-rater location against its generating value condition by condition; audit the centering constraint; and disentangle the sample-size confound (N, rater count, and effect-rater count move together). If a one-line centering fix corrects it, document that transparently (Ch. 2). Effort: low–medium.



8.2.2 Tier 2 — Re-operationalize the manipulations

(d) Replace α = 0 “misfit” with a realistic range. A zero slope is a uniform random rater, not a noisy human; use a slope gradient (e.g., α ∈ {0.8, 0.5, 0.2}) plus a halo or central-tendency mechanism, and add a neutral cross-generative cell so neither model is graded on home turf (Ch. 3). Effort: higher — new simulation cells, same framework.
(e) Re-manipulate local dependence on the residual scale. Compute the realized residual correlation per condition; the marginal LI baseline depends on the unit (≈ 0.79 per total score, ≈ 0.43 per item), so 0.2 injects negative residual dependence while 0.6 is a genuine positive violation under the “among items” reading (0.9 on either) — re-manipulate on the residual scale (a shared rater-pair random effect or copula) and re-scope the “MFRM is robust” claim to the conditions that are genuine violations (Ch. 6). Effort: medium.



8.2.3 Tier 3 — Housekeeping

(f) Reconcile the condition count and release the log. Resolve whether the grid is 3⁴ = 81 or 4 × 3³ = 108, trace the stray n = 900, and make the condition log / simulation code available as supplementary material (Ch. 7). Effort: low.
(g) Analyze at the replicate level. Replace per-indicator ANOVAs that ignore clustering with replicate-level summaries or a mixed-effects model, and either justify η² ≥ .01 against a detection baseline or replace it (Ch. 7). Effort: low–medium.
(h) Double-code ~20% of the systematic-review articles. Have a second coder apply the scheme independently and report Cohen’s κ (or Krippendorff’s α) — additive, but expected (Ch. 7). Effort: low.

title: 8  A minimal-revision roadmap (and the questions to expect)
text:
8.3 The questions to expect, gathered







Chapter
The sharpest question a reviewer will ask




1 — ψ identifiability
“In what sense is a reported ψ of 16–32 an estimate, given the profile likelihood is flat for ψ ≳ 5 — and why average a non-estimable quantity into an ANOVA?”


2 — Location collapse
“Why does the severe-rater location fall monotonically with N when more data should stabilize it? Show recovery vs. true values, condition by condition.”


3 — Misfit operationalization
“Is a slope of 0 (uniform noise) a valid model of inaccuracy, and how do you separate the MFRM’s generate-equals-fit advantage from a real finding?”


4 — MCMC adequacy
“What are the ESS and MCSE of φ and ψ at 700 draws from one chain — are the interpreted differences actually larger than the MCSE? How was R-hat computed from one chain?”


5 — Sensitivity definition
“Your definition is detection — why report η² of means on effect raters only, not TPR/FPR/ROC-AUC at your cut? And why is η² ≥ .01 ‘practical’ when it is near-chance?”


6 — Dependence scale
“The marginal LI baseline depends on the unit (≈ 0.79 per total score, ≈ 0.43 per item); under the ‘among items’ reading, 0.2 is below it (not a violation) while 0.6 and 0.9 are genuine violations. Which scale were the targets on — and can a null be read as robustness? Report the residual correlation per condition.”


7 — Housekeeping
“Are there 81 or 108 conditions — can you supply the full grid? And what is the inter-coder reliability for the systematic-review coding?”

title: 8  A minimal-revision roadmap (and the questions to expect)
text:
8.4 Why this is worth doing
Each item on this list is a methodological strength waiting to be unlocked. Reviewers do not reward authors who anticipated no criticism; they reward authors who have already thought the critique through and answered it in the manuscript. Reporting ROC-AUC beside η² is not a concession of weakness — it demonstrates command of the detection-theoretic foundations of the construct. Running four chains and reporting R-hat is not an apology for the dissertation — it demonstrates what adequate MCMC looks like. Documenting the centering audit turns a potential confound into a finding in its own right. The revised studies stop being merely plausible — where a reader must trust the setup — and become persuasive, where a skeptical reader can check the work and find it standing.
The hardest part is already done: two original simulation studies were designed and run, a systematic review completed, and a full dissertation written. The revisions ahead are, by comparison, targeted and finite — and every simulation study in the top measurement journals went through exactly this kind of sharpening. The architecture of the work is sound, and the central conclusions are reachable from it.






TipThe eight-item checklist




(a) Ch. 5 — Report TPR / FPR / ROC-AUC (+ recovery); include normal and effect raters; retire or justify η² ≥ .01
(b) Ch. 1 + 4 — Re-run HRM: ≥ 4 chains, longer warm-up, report R-hat / ESS / MCSE, prior sensitivity on ψ, flag ψ non-estimable for noise raters
(c) Ch. 2 — Recovery (bias / RMSE / coverage) for severe-rater location per condition; centering audit; disentangle the N–rater-count confound
(d) Ch. 3 — Replace α = 0 with a slope gradient + realistic mechanisms; add neutral cross-generative cells
(e) Ch. 6 — Report realized residual correlation per condition; re-manipulate on the residual scale; re-scope the “robust” claim
(f) Ch. 7 — Reconcile 81 vs. 108 (+ n = 900); release the condition log
(g) Ch. 7 — Replicate-level / mixed-effects analysis; justify or replace η² ≥ .01
(h) Ch. 7 — Double-code ~20% of the systematic-review articles; report Cohen’s κ

title: Appendix A — Reproducibility appendix
text:
A.1 How the checks were built
Every number in this book is reproducible from code. This appendix explains how the checks were built, states the verification standard, and lists the shared tools and the environment.
Each chapter’s figure runs a small simulation that implements the dissertation’s own equations — the PC-MFRM data-generating model (Eqs. 7/8), the MFRM infit/outfit fit statistics (Eqs. 2/3), the GPCM first stage of the HRM (Eq. 5), and the signal-detection second stage (Eq. 6) — coded from scratch in R. No chapter re-runs TAM or immer. The aim is to show how the model as specified behaves, so the argument never depends on reproducing a particular software run.
Those shared equations live in one file, R/common.R, which every chapter sources. The implementation was cross-checked against an independent Python reference written from the same equations: on a grid of 610 probability cells plus the expected score, the score variance, and seven scalar cases (log-likelihoods and fit statistics), the R and Python implementations agree to ≈ 2 × 10⁻¹⁴ — machine precision. So any difference between a chapter’s R figure and that reference comes only from Monte-Carlo draws, never from the arithmetic.

title: Appendix A — Reproducibility appendix
text:
A.2 The verification standard
Each quantity a chapter relies on is one of two kinds:

Deterministic (a seed-independent analytic fact — e.g., a slope of 0 gives exactly uniform category probabilities; the SDM tends to uniform as ψ → ∞; the false-positive rate at the +0.5 cut is \(1-\Phi(1)=0.159\); an \(\eta^2\) of .01 maps to AUC \(\approx 0.557\)). These must match the independent reference essentially exactly.
Stochastic (a Monte-Carlo estimate — e.g., the recovered severe-rater location, the baseline marginal correlation, the empirical false-positive rate). These match within Monte-Carlo tolerance; R’s RNG differs from NumPy’s, so the exact draws differ while the conclusion does not.

The frozen reference values, with each quantity’s type and tolerance, were fixed in advance, and every claim was checked against the page and quotation it rests on in the source dissertation.

title: Appendix A — Reproducibility appendix
text:
A.4 Random seeds and cross-checks
Each chapter’s figure sets an explicit seed (visible in its code block), so every stochastic quantity reproduces exactly on re-render. During development, each derived value was also cross-checked against an independent Python implementation of the same equations.
